3.1.2.1. The Contractor’s updated PCP shall address the management of operating supplies to include expendable items and consumable items. The Contractor shall manage expendable and consumable items as GFM, not personal property. Expendable items have a service life of less than two years and are operating supplies. Consumable items such as paper products, cleaning supplies, office supplies, fast-moving petroleum inventories (Class III), work stock, bench stock, dining facility plastic table wares are operating supplies. Operating supplies are GFM. Criticality: MANDATORY. Modal verb: shall.

3.1.5.2. Transition Technical Inspections (TI) and Reporting: The incoming Contractor shall coordinate with the COR and outgoing Contractor on all completed and pending TI. The incoming Contractor shall immediately begin replacements, repairs and maintenance on all workloads. The incoming Contractor shall complete needed TI’s and request disposition from the Government within 15 calendar days of the Contractor’s Task Order Responsibility Date (CTORD) with zero discrepancies allowed. The Government will provide the requested disposition(s) within 15 calendar days. Criticality: MANDATORY. Modal verb: shall. Due date: within 15 calendar days of CTORD. Threshold: zero discrepancies.

inspection, what task order areas will be inspected, how discrepancies will be identified and what actions will be taken to correct and preclude recurrence. Plans that are generic, lack TO specific details, or appear to be simply re-branded versions of the basic contract Quality Control Plan will be deemed unacceptable. This quality control plan will fulfil CDRL A014. 

The following combined technical/risk rating shall be used in the technical evaluation of Technical Subfactor 1.1: 

$^ * \mathrm { A }$ strength is defined as the following: An aspect of the proposal that exceeds the specified requirement in a way that provides benefit to the government. 

$^ { * } \mathrm { A }$ weakness is defined as the following: a flaw in the proposal that increases the risk of unsuccessful contract performance. 

$^ { * } \mathrm { A }$ deficiency is defined as the following: a material failure of a proposal to meet a Government requirement or a combination of significant weaknesses in a proposal that increases the risk of unsuccessful contract performance to an unacceptable level. 

The following ratings shall be used in the technical evaluation for Subfactors 1.2, 1.3, and 1.4: 

$^ { * } \mathrm { A }$ risk is defined as the following: the potential for unsuccessful contract performance. The consideration of risk assesses the degree to which an Offeror’s proposed approach to achieving the technical factor or subfactor may involve risk of disruption of schedule, degradation of performance, the need for increased Government oversight, and the likelihood of unsuccessful contract performance. 

$^ * \mathrm { A }$ deficiency is defined as the following: a material failure of a proposal to meet a Government requirement or a combination of requirements in a proposal that increases the risk of unsuccessful contract performance to an unacceptable level. 

Any technical subfactor evaluated as Unacceptable will render the entire proposal as overall Unacceptable and, therefore, un-awardable. 

11. EVALUATION FACTOR 2: Price. Adequate price competition is anticipated and exists if two or more offers are received that satisfy the Government’s requirements. Therefore, the contractors’ total evaluated prices (TEPs) will be evaluated for price reasonableness. The TEP will be the only discriminator amongst contractors for this factor. Price analysis shall be used to evaluate the price reasonableness of each offeror’s TEP. 

As per FAR 52.217-8, the Government may extend performance of any services unilaterally. The option to extend under this clause may be exercised at any point during the life of the contract and may be exercised more than once, however, the total extension of performance under this option shall not exceed six months. The contractor shall submit a monthly price for the six-month extension for all applicable CLINS (Attachment 2). 

The Government may compare contractor TEPs, compare TEPs to the Independent Government Estimate (IGE), or may use other price analysis techniques per FAR 16.505(b)(3). 

In the event only one offer is received, the Government will not perform a best value evaluation of the proposal, but rather will evaluate the Offeror’s proposal for technical acceptability. In accordance with DFARS 216.505-70, the Government will transition from a competitive process to a sole source acquisition and subsequently evaluate the proposal price/costs to determine a fair and reasonable price. The Government may or may not enter into negotiations with the sole Offeror. Evaluation of option price(s) shall not obligate the Government to exercise the option(s). For purposes of evaluation, the TEP is defined in Attachment 2. 

The Government will also perform a price realism analysis on the Contractor’s proposed USN labor rates. Offeror’s proposal shall include a detailed labor pricing breakdown (Attachment 2) that includes the proposed labor category, number of Full Time Equivalent (FTE) positions, total hours, and the burdened and unburdened hourly labor rates for each USN labor category. The unburdened labor rate is defined as the total compensation paid to an employee to include location uplifts and bonuses, etc. The unburdened labor rate should reflect only the employee’s compensation; that is not to include overhead, fringe, G&A, profit, etc. The burdened labor rate is defined as the employee compensation (i.e., unburdened rate) to include the addition of applicable overhead, fringe, G&A, and profit rates, etc. The Government will determine the risk associated with each labor category by using any of the following techniques: market research, comparison to the IGE, comparison to other offerors, historical rates paid under AFCAP V, etc. If the Government determines the rates are too low, resulting in the offeror’s likely inability to hire and retain personnel to meet PWS requirements, then the proposal may be deemed not awardable, even if the proposal is determined acceptable or above from a technical standpoint. 

If, after receipt of proposals, there is insufficient information available to determine price reasonableness and/or price realism, offerors may be required to submit other than certified cost or pricing data. The FAR does not preclude the requirement for obtaining other than certified cost or pricing data and the Government reserves the right to obtain data as appropriate. Shall the evaluation change to a non-competitive/sole source process, certified cost and pricing data shall be required as directed by policy. 

12. Serco, Inc. and Huntington Ingalls Industries to include all subsidiaries (i.e. Camber Corporation) are excluded from proposing as a subcontractor on this requirement due to involvement with 

requirement development and access to AFCAP V contractor proprietary information. 

13. KMSCo are excluded now from any future USAF award consideration as a prime or subcontractor. 

14. All information required by this FOPR shall be sent to the following addresses via DOD SAFE or email: 

15. Questions to this FOPR shall be sent to the addresses indicated in number 15 above, as well as to the following addresses via DOD SAFE or email: 

16. Contractors are required to complete the appropriate boxes, sign and date the attached Representation by Corporations as applicable (Attachment 3). 

17. Should you have any questions in regards to this FOPR, please contact SSgt Keymyn Hughes via email at keymyn.hughes@us.af.mil, SSgt Austin Grabowski at austin.grabowski@us.af.mil, and Mr. Ellery English at ellery.english@us.af.mil. 

Attachments: 

1. PWS dated 12 November 2025   
2. CLIN Structure Price Schedule dated 12 December 2025   
3. Additional FAR Provisions and Clauses 31 December 2025   
4. Questions Template Structure   
5. Staffing Matrix Template 

Exhibit A: CDRL List -- see Appendix E in PWS

FROM: 772 ESS/PKD 139 Barnes Drive Suite 1 Tyndall Air Force Base (AFB), FL 32403-5319 

SUBJECT: Fair Opportunity Proposal Request (FOPR), FA8051-26-R-1008: TRANSIENT AIRCRAFT SERVICES, MULT-LOCATIONS, Al Udeid Air Base (AUAB), Al Dhafra Air Base (ADAB), Ali Al Salem Air Base (AASAB), Muwaffaq Salti Air Base (MSAB), Prince Sultan Air Base (PSAB) 

1. This is a competitive fair opportunity selection conducted in accordance with (IAW) the Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) Part 16.505(b)(1). Orders placed under FAR 16.505 indefinite-delivery indefinite-quantity (IDIQ) contracts are not source selections and are not subject to FAR 15.3 procedures. Accordingly, issuance of a Task Order (TO) under this FOPR, should an award be made, shall follow the ordering procedures outlined in FAR 16.505, as supplemented, and the AFCAP V ordering procedures. The FOPR for the subject requirement is issued IAW the attached Performance Work Statement (PWS) (Attachment 1), as well as the terms and conditions of the AFCAP V, IDIQ basic contract requirements as stated in the FOPR and attachments. All claimed capabilities to meet the requirements shall be realistic and are subject to verification by the Government. The Government intends to award a Firm-fixed-price Task Order (TO) for the subject project. Notice to Offeror(s)/Supplier(s): Funds are not presently available for this effort. No award will be made under this solicitation until funds are available. The Government reserves the right to cancel this solicitation, either before or after the closing date. In the event the Government cancels this solicitation, the Government has no obligation to reimburse an offeror for any costs. 

The Government intends to make an award based on the initial proposal submissions without conducting interchanges. Therefore, each offer should contain the offeror’s best terms from a price and technical standpoint. However, the Government reserves the right to hold interchanges if during the evaluation, it is determined to be in the best interest of the Government. Interchanges are fluid interaction(s) between the Contracting Officer (CO) and the Offerors that may address any aspect of the proposal and may or may not be documented in real time. Offeror responses to interchanges shall be considered in making the order selection decision. Interchanges may be conducted with one, some, or all offerors as the Government is not required to conduct interchanges with any or all contractors responding to this FOPR. If the Government enters interchanges, the Government may or may not request final proposal revisions. All documents shall be submitted through the DOD SAFE File Exchange (https://safe.apps.mil/) or via e-mail to the appropriate point of contact identified in this FOPR. It is the offeror’s responsibility to ensure the Government receives all documents by the deadline. 

2. A site visit will not be held for this requirement. 

3. Questions shall be submitted by the contractor and received by 772 ESS/PKD no later than 12 January 2026 at 0900 CST (Local Tyndall Time). Questions shall be submitted on a word document in the exact format provided at attachment four and sent via DOD SAFE or email. If questions are received after the date and time identified in this FOPR, the Government may or may not provide answers. 

4. Proposals are due 30 January 2026 at 0900 CST (Local Tyndall Time). Proposals shall be valid for 120 days after proposal due date. 

5. The anticipated award date for this TO is 5 March 2026. 

6. Assumptions and exceptions: Contractors shall address contractor’s assumptions and proposed exceptions to the CO through the questions and answers process prior to proposal submittal. Proposals shall not be considered if the proposal contains any assumptions and/or exceptions. 

7. The anticipated period of performance for this TO is as follows: 

\*Base Year includes five (5) calendar days of transition. The contractor shall have all personnel on-site at the beginning of the period of performance/transition period which begins 21 May 2026. The Contractor Task Order Responsibility Date (CTORD) is 26 May 2026. 

NOTE: The current AUAB, AASAB, ADAB, MSAB Task Order OY is: 26 May 2025 – 25 May 2026. 

8. The format for proposals shall be as follows: The proposals shall be on $8 . 5 ^ { \prime \prime } \times 1 1 ^ { \prime \prime }$ paper. A page is defined as one (1) face of a sheet of paper containing information. The type shall not be less than 12 point font. The contractor shall prepare the proposal as set forth in the proposal organization table (Table 1). The titles and contents of the section shall be as defined in this table, all of which shall be within the required page limits as specified in Table 1. For the CLIN Structure Price Schedule (Attachment 2), all proposed prices, units, and totals shall be whole dollar amounts, with no decimals. The cover letter shall include offerors’ contract number, a statement that the company understands the requirements specified, and will meet the performance standards and requirements therein, and a statement that the company does not take exception to any of the requirements of this order. Each individual proposal section/volume, as outlined in the table below, shall be submitted as separate files clearly identifying the individual volumes. The contents of each proposal section are described in the FOPR as noted in Table 1 below: 

9. Evaluation Criteria: The CO will comply with procedures specified in the basic contract to ensure fair opportunity is provided to all AFCAP V contractors. The award will be made based on tradeoff procedures for two evaluation factors: Technical Subfactor $1 . 1 - \mathrm { T O M P }$ , and Factor 2 – Price. An award will be made to the contractor proposing the combination most advantageous to the 

Government based on an integrated assessment of the tradeoff evaluation factors. The relative importance of each tradeoff factor is as follows: Technical Subfactor 1.1 – TOMP is more important than Factor 2 - Price. 

Factor 1 – Technical Subfactors 1.2, 1.3, and 1.4 are not part of the tradeoff process. Factor 1 – Technical Subfactors 1.2, 1.3, and 1.4 shall be evaluated as Acceptable/Unacceptable. To be awardable, the offeror must have an Acceptable rating for Technical Subfactors 1.2., 1.3., and 1.4 in Factor 1 - Technical. 

Factor 2 - Price is evaluated for reasonableness, and United States National (USN) Labor realism. 

The CO shall make an integrated best value award decision in accordance with the basis for award. While the Government will strive for maximum objectivity, the selection process, by its nature, is subjective; and therefore, professional judgement is implicit throughout the entire process. The offeror’s proposal should not simply rephrase or restate the Government’s requirements. The elements to be addressed by the offeror include the following: 

10. EVALUATION FACTOR 1: Technical Factor. 

a. Subfactor 1.1: Task Order Management Plan (TOMP) (CDRL A002). The contractor shall provide a plan that mirrors and expands on the contractor’s AFCAP V Management Plan while demonstrating how the contractor will fulfill the specific requirements for this task order. The plan shall include a designation of an empowered on-site manager(s) with the ability to make decisions and direct all sub-contractors on behalf of the prime contractor and all subcontracting services necessary to maintain a capability to perform under this requirement. Proposals shall clearly identify all subcontract personnel and teaming partner(s), to include the company name of the teaming partner or subcontractor, the number of hours proposed for each subcontract position and labor category. The TOMP shall address all requirements for operation at the applicable site(s) and function(s) listed in the PWS, all associated appendices, annexes, and attachments. The TOMP shall include all equipment, labor, labor hours and materials incidental to the contractor’s housing, transportation, utilities, facilities, services or other systems, in addition to the support provided to the designated force. The TOMP shall address all current Foreign Clearance Guide, Combatant Command, installation, and Host Nation requirements for entry and access to, and operations at, the Task Order site. Contractors shall provide relevant copies of permissions, licenses, approvals, detailed plans to obtain licenses, etc. to conduct business in the relevant country, location, etc. A plan shall outline process milestones to meet the CTORD, license support partners, if applicable, and any other relevant and/or past information supporting the contractor’s ability to obtain all applicable licenses. The contractor must provide substantiating documentation demonstrating the firm's ability to conduct business in the place(s) of performance under this TO, this substantiation will not count towards the page limitation. 

The contractor shall propose their mobilization plan and schedule. The contractor shall develop a mobilization schedule showing each activity, their relationships, duration, critical path, associated critical risks, and planned mitigation. Milestones in the mobilization schedule shall be based on timeframes (ex. Award $+ 1 0$ calendar days) and not specific dates. The contractor must demonstrate their ability to successfully mobilize, transition, and demobilize, with mobilization/transition being complete and the CTORD no later than 0001 on 26 May 2026. The contractor shall provide a detailed schedule that shall include (at a minimum): activation, subcontracting, transportation, mobilization, transition, demobilization plan, logistical support, surveying, site work, site visits, critical path and any other significant activity. The mobilization plan shall explain and detail compliance with all applicable travel restrictions and ensure necessary timelines have been included and accounted for to include all Foreign Clearance Guide and other Combatant Command and country access requirements. The plan will further describe the timelines and processes for acceptance and turnover of all information, property, and equipment. The contractor shall propose their mobilization schedule timelines. 

The timeline will reflect milestones the contractor deems necessary, but will also at a minimum reflect the following: 

a. When $50 \%$ of the contractor’s deploying U.S. National personnel will have completed deployment processing at the Deployment Processing Center as designated in their AFCAP basic contract.   
b. When $50 \%$ of the contractor’s workforce (U.S. National, Local National (if applicable) and Other Country Nationals (if applicable)), have arrived on-site.   
c. When $100 \%$ of the contractor’s deploying U.S. National personnel have completed deployment processing at the Deployment Processing Center as designated in their AFCAP basic contract.   
d. When $100 \%$ of the contractor’s workforce (U.S. National, Local National (if applicable) and Other Country Nationals (if applicable)) have arrived on-site. 

The offeror must provide a staffing plan including an Organizational Structure Chart and Staffing Matrix with narrative that fully accounts for all the manpower by functional area, listing the proposed time labor hours by full-time equivalent, to clearly meet all PWS requirements and explain how they will manage the effort. The staffing plan must also demonstrate that proposed personnel will meet any minimum qualifications and experience requirements outlined in the PWS. The staffing plan does not count toward the page count limitations of the FOPR. The staffing matrix shall be submitted in the format provided at attachment five and sent via DOD SAFE or email. The staffing matrix shall include the quantity and type of personnel (United States Nationals (USNs)/Local Nationals (LNs)/Other Country Nations (OCNs), quantity of paid labor hours per person (daily, weekly, annually) grouped by skill/trade/discipline, physical location (if applicable), total task order proposed paid labor hours for each service area, each performance period and summed for the task order as a whole. 

The contractor may use 10 font only for the mobilization/transition (if applicable) and staffing plan with $1 1 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { ~ x ~ } 1 7 ^ { \circ }$ page size and landscaped format. This TOMP will fulfill CDRL A002. This subfactor will receive a combined technical/risk rating; the combined rating will be based on the contractor’s demonstration of providing a TOMP that will adequately meet all of the PWS requirements in accordance with Table 2 below. 

b. Subfactor 1.2: Mission Essential Plan (MEP). Acceptability of this subfactor shall be based on the contractor’s demonstrated ability and planned strategy to continue contractor services during a crisis situation for up to 30 days or until normal operations can resume. For the Government to make this determination, the contractor shall provide with its proposal, a written plan describing how it will continue to perform the mission essential contractor services listed in the PWS, during periods of crisis. This plan will become part of the TO at time of award. 

1) Identify provisions made for the acquisition of essential personnel and resources, if necessary, for continuity of operations for up to 30 days or until normal operations can be resumed; 

2) Address in the plan, at a minimum— 

i. Challenges associated with maintaining essential contractor services during an extended event, such as a pandemic that occurs in repeated waves;   
ii. The time lapse associated with the initiation of the acquisition of essential personnel and resources and their actual availability on site;   
iii. The components, processes, and requirements for the identification, training, and preparedness of personnel who are capable of relocating to alternate facilities or performing work from home;   
iv. Any established alert and notification procedures for mobilizing identified “essential contractor service” personnel; and   
v. The approach for communicating expectations to contractor employees regarding their roles and responsibilities during a crisis. 

c. Subfactor 1.3: Combating Trafficking in Persons (CTIP) Compliance Plan. Contractors must provide a CTIP compliance plan as outlined in FAR 52.222-50 Combating Trafficking in Persons. This plan will become part of the TO at time of award. Acceptability will be based on meeting the minimum requirements of the FAR clause outlined in paragraph (h)(3) as depicted below. This requirement is applicable to all contracts for all supplies and services. This clause is applicable to both prime and sub-contractors performing under this task order. This plan does not count toward the page count limitations of the FOPR. The compliance plan must include, at a minimum, the following: 

i. An awareness program to inform contractor employees about the Government’s policy prohibiting trafficking-related activities described in paragraph (b) of this clause, the activities prohibited, and the actions that will be taken against the employee for violations. Additional information about Trafficking in Persons and examples of awareness programs can be found at the website for the Department of State’s Office to Monitor and Combat Trafficking in Persons at https://www.state.gov/bureaus-offices/under-secretary-for-foreign-assistancehumanitarian-affairs-and-religious-freedom/bureau-of-democracy-humanrights-and-labor/office-to-monitor-and-combat-trafficking-in-persons/. 

ii. A process for employees to report, without fear of retaliation, activity inconsistent with the policy prohibiting trafficking in persons, including a means to make available to all employees the hotline phone number of the Global Human Trafficking Hotline at 1-844-888-FREE and its email address at help@befree.org. 

iii. A recruitment and wage plan that only permits the use of recruitment companies with trained employees prohibits charging recruitment fees to the employees or potential employees and ensures that wages meet applicable host-country legal requirements or explains any variance. 

iv. A housing plan, if the Contractor or subcontractor intends to provide or arrange housing that ensures that the housing meets host-country housing and safety standards. 

v. Procedures to prevent agents and subcontractors at any tier and at any dollar value from engaging in trafficking in persons (including activities in paragraph (b) of this clause) and to monitor, detect, and terminate any agents, subcontracts, or subcontractor employees that have engaged in such activities. 

d. Subfactor 1.4: Task Order Specific Quality Control Plan (CDRL A014). Proposals shall include a task order specific quality control plan including examples of task order specific quality inspection documentation (e.g., metrics, reports, etc.). The plan shall provide information on the methods of inspection, what task order areas will be inspected, how discrepancies will be identified and what actions will be taken to correct and preclude recurrence. Plans that are generic, lack TO specific details, or appear to be simply re-branded versions of the basic contract Quality Control Plan will be deemed unacceptable. This quality control plan will fulfil CDRL A014. 

The following combined technical/risk rating shall be used in the technical evaluation of Technical Subfactor 1.1: 

$^ * \mathrm { A }$ strength is defined as the following: An aspect of the proposal that exceeds the specified requirement in a way that provides benefit to the government. 

$^ { * } \mathrm { A }$ weakness is defined as the following: a flaw in the proposal that increases the risk of unsuccessful contract performance. 

$^ { * } \mathrm { A }$ deficiency is defined as the following: a material failure of a proposal to meet a Government requirement or a combination of significant weaknesses in a proposal that increases the risk of unsuccessful contract performance to an unacceptable level. 

The following ratings shall be used in the technical evaluation for Subfactors 1.2, 1.3, and 1.4: 

$^ { * } \mathrm { A }$ risk is defined as the following: the potential for unsuccessful contract performance. The consideration of risk assesses the degree to which an Offeror’s proposed approach to achieving the technical factor or subfactor may involve risk of disruption of schedule, degradation of performance, the need for increased Government oversight, and the likelihood of unsuccessful contract performance. 

$^ * \mathrm { A }$ deficiency is defined as the following: a material failure of a proposal to meet a Government requirement or a combination of requirements in a proposal that increases the risk of unsuccessful contract performance to an unacceptable level. 

Any technical subfactor evaluated as Unacceptable will render the entire proposal as overall Unacceptable and, therefore, un-awardable. 

11. EVALUATION FACTOR 2: Price. Adequate price competition is anticipated and exists if two or more offers are received that satisfy the Government’s requirements. Therefore, the contractors’ total evaluated prices (TEPs) will be evaluated for price reasonableness. The TEP will be the only discriminator amongst contractors for this factor. Price analysis shall be used to evaluate the price reasonableness of each offeror’s TEP. 

As per FAR 52.217-8, the Government may extend performance of any services unilaterally. The option to extend under this clause may be exercised at any point during the life of the contract and may be exercised more than once, however, the total extension of performance under this option shall not exceed six months. The contractor shall submit a monthly price for the six-month extension for all applicable CLINS (Attachment 2).

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS) TRANSIENT AIRCRAFT SERVICES, MULTI-LOCATIONS, SOUTHWEST ASIA (SWA) AIR FORCE CENTRAL COMMAND (USAFCENT), SHAW AFB, SC 12 November 2025 

1.0. DESCRIPTION OF SERVICES. In accordance with (IAW) the Air Force Contract Augmentation Program (AFCAP) V Basic Contract, the Contractor shall provide all personnel, supervision, services and items not provided as Government Furnished Equipment (GFE) or Government Furnished Property (GFP) necessary to operate Transient Aircraft Services (TAS) (Fixed and Rotary Wing Aircraft) in support of base operations at specific locations within the Southwest Asia region. The Government will provide everything directly supporting servicing aircraft with the exception of PPE/safety gear and uniforms. The Government requires some or all of the services at the following locations:Al Udeid Air Base (AUAB), Qatar (Appendix G); Al Dhafra Air Base (ADAB), United Arab Emirates (U.A.E.) (Appendix H); Ali Al Salem Air Base (AASAB), Kuwait (Appendix I); Muwaffaq Salti Air Base (MSAB), Jordan (Appendix J); Prince Sultan Air Base (PSAB), Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (K.S.A.) (Appendix K); and Al Jaber Air Base, (AJAB), Kuwait.(Appendix L). Location unique/specific requirements are detailed in the listed appendices. The Contractor shall perform to the standards in this contract. Due to flight line security regulations, only United States Nationals (USNs) are allowed to perform services on this task. 

1.1. SCOPE. Unless otherwise stated, the Contractor shall provide all personnel, supervision, labor, training, tools, safety equipment and other items and services necessary to perform TAS services supporting United States (U.S.) and Coalition Forces as described in this PWS and its appendices. The Contractor shall function as an integral team member, cooperating with and supporting U.S. deployed forces and shall share information with other integral team members, such as Government personnel and other contractors, as required. Unless otherwise indicated, the requirements in this PWS are mission essential.The Contractor shall provide TAS services as described in Appendix F and location specific appendices. 

1.2. TASKS: In addition to the AFCAP V Basic contract, section 1.2, the following requirements are specific to this task order (TO): 

1.2.1. Task Order Management Plan (TOMP). [CDRL A002]: 

1.2.1.1. . The Contractor shall submit a TOMP as part of the initial task order proposal and provide subsequent updates as necessary. The Contractor shall update the TOMP to reflect current requirements and planned execution. The Contractor shall submit updated TOMPs with proposals for modifications or whenever significant changes occur. Upon award of the initial task order and any subsequent modifications, the current TOMP will be incorporated into the TO. Updates must be approved by the Procuring Contracting Officer (PCO), in consultation with AFCAP Program Management and the customer prior to implementation. The Government will use the incorporated TOMP as a baseline for equitable adjustments (EA) in the event of Contractor non-performance. 

1.2.1.2. The Contractor shall provide a TOMP that mirrors and expands on the Contractor’s AFCAP V Management Plan while demonstrating how the Contractor will fulfill the specific requirements for this task order. 

1.2.1.3. The TOMP shall clearly identify all proposed key personnel and their availability IAW PWS paragraph 1.2.3. to meet all PWS requirements. The TOMP shall designate an empowered site manager with the ability to make decisions and direct all sub-contractors on behalf of the prime contractor and all subcontracting services necessary to maintain a capability to perform under this requirement. 

1.2.1.4. The TOMP shall address all operation requirements at the applicable sites and service areas listed in the PWS. 

1.2.1.5. The TOMP shall include all equipment, labor and materials incidental to the contractor’s transportation, utilities, facilities, services and other systems supporting the designated force. 

1.2.1.6. The Contractor shall incorporate Host Nation (HN) requirements as an exhibit into the TOMP. The Contractor shall provide proof of current HN authorization to perform this TO at the given location and shall maintain authorization to perform for the duration of this TO. 

1.2.1.7. The TOMP shall include: 

1.2.1.7.1. The mobilization and demobilization plan shall clearly outline all required predeployment training, including the issuance and return of necessary equipment (e.g., from the Expeditionary Theater Distribution Center (ETDC)). The TOMP shall demonstrate compliance with applicable reporting instructions and travel restrictions, and identify clear timelines for meeting all Foreign Clearance Guide (FCG) and other Combatant Command and country access requirements before deployment training. The Contractor shall tailor the plan specific to the requirements for the specific locations as identified in this PWS. The Contractor shall comply with all travel clearance requirements IAW PWS Paragraph 4.4. The mobilization/transition plan shall further describe the timelines and processes for acceptance and turnover of all information and equipment to include any necessary Technical Inspections (TI) IAW PWS Paragraph 3.1.5.2. See CDRL A002 for additional details. 

1.2.1.7.2. The Contractor shall demonstrate the ability to successfully mobilize and demobilize, within the required timelines. Following TO award but before the kickoff meeting, the Contractor shall update all proposed timelines by replacing “task order award plus X number of days” with actual calendar dates reflecting the mobilization schedule incorporated into the contract. The Contractor shall develop and include in the initial TOMP a risk mitigation plan to address staffing risks (to include performance of requirements during contractor personnel absences (e.g., vacancies, rest and recreation (R&R) and emergency leave), and risks associated with equipment or materials not onsite or anticipated by the start of the Transition Period. 

1.2.1.7.3. The Contractor shall provide the demobilization plan for Administrative Contracting Officer (ACO) acceptance no later than (NLT) 60 calendar days prior to scheduled contract expiration/termination. The demobilization plan shall clearly indicate property accountability processes, e.g., RPIE, CWDE, PPE/IPE, to include reimbursement for lost or damaged property. 

1.2.1.7.4. The Contractor shall provide updated mobilization and demobilization plans to the PCO, ACO, and Contracting Officer Representative (COR) for acceptance NLT ten (10) business days after the Kickoff meeting. The Contractor shall provide the demobilization plan to the PCO, ACO, and COR for acceptance NLT 30 calendar days before the scheduled departure of personnel at any of the site locations. 

1.2.1.7.5. The TOMP shall provide a staffing matrix that includes the quantity and type of personnel (United States Nationals (USNs)/Local Nationals (LNs)/Other Country Nationals (OCNs), quantity of paid labor hours per person (daily, weekly, annually) grouped by skill/trade/discipline, physical location (if applicable), total TO proposed paid labor hours for each service area, each performance period, and summed for the TO as a whole. The labor hours shall be identified only as the paid hours proposed for a labor category, a service area, each performance period and for the TO as a whole. The TOMP shall clearly identify the number of paid hours proposed for each employee and labor category and provide an organizational chart delineating span of control that clearly defines/demonstrates an organizational staffing plan and qualified subcontractor/teaming partner(s) that meet all PWS requirements. The TOMP should clearly indicate any proposed cross-utilization of personnel across various service areas or skills/trades/disciplines. Unless otherwise allowed elsewhere in this PWS, all services shall be provided by the onsite personnel in the disciplines identified. The Contractor’s proposed staffing matrix plan, particularly for key personnel and technical leads, shall be clearly indicated by intermediate and completion milestones leading up to CTORD. The TOMP shall also identify all subcontract labor support and/or subcontractor teaming partner(s), to include the company name of the teaming partner or subcontractor, the number of hours proposed for each subcontract position and labor category. There shall be zero discrepancies in the TOMP data. 

1.2.1.7.6. The TOMP shall demonstrate the Contractor’s plan to comply with Synchronized Predeployment and Operational Tracker (SPOT) requirements. SPOT is the federal Government’s database for the tracking and accountability of contractor personnel and equipment during contingencies. SPOT provides a centralized means to identify the Department’s reliance and dependence on contracted capabilities. SPOT offers fidelity and visibility of existing and previously contracted support, so that planners and others may consider current and historical data when developing future total force structure requirements. All USNs, Local Nationals (LNs) and Other Country Nationals (OCNs) assigned to and working on this TO will require a SPOT record to include subcontractors at all tiers. A Letter of Authorization (LOA) is required for personnel authorized Government Furnished Life Support amenities. SPOT requirements to be addressed in the TOMP: 

1.2.1.7.6.1. All USNs, LNs and OCNs assigned to this task shall have a record in SPOT, a record of deployment and an LOA if applicable. 

1.2.1.7.6.2. The individual’s arrival date in theater and duty location assignment shall be confirmed in SPOT within 24 hours of arrival. 

1.2.1.7.6.3. Records/deployments shall be closed out within three (3) calendar days of resignation or termination and the Government Deployment Manager notified. 

1.2.1.7.6.4. The correct Foreign Identification Number (FIN) for LNs and OCNs shall be used or follow the SPOT Business Rules if a FIN is not available. 

1.2.1.7.6.5. An individual’s change in employment status (e.g., R&R, missing, or injured, but intends to return to duty/work) shall be updated in SPOT within 24 hours of the change in status. 

1.2.2. Transition/Mobilization/CTORD: During the mobilization/transition period and until full staffing is achieved, the Contractor shall submit a weekly staffing progress report to the PCO, AFCAP Program Manager, ACO, and COR. The report shall show all proposed positions’ status [CDRL A011]. The report shall provide staffing progress indicating all proposed positions, personnel, recruitment status, planned deployment training, equipment issue, planned and actual date of onsite arrival, and any relevant comments about on-site mobilization progress. 

1.2.2.1. The Contractor shall provide a transition into CTORD at all service locations. Transition shall beginning five (5) calendar days prior to start of the scheduled CTORD IAW the TOMP schedule. This required transition period will enable the incoming service provider management to coordinate with the current military personnel and/or incumbent contractor, as applicable on the numerous details necessary to start CTORD. The Government will facilitate on-site training during the transition period with the Contractor. This will include local standards and procedures and overall familiarization of the operations. No other transition support will be provided. 

1.2.2.2. After the CTORD date, the contractor shall provide weekly updates of deployed personnel [CDRL A013]. This report is in addition to the weekly staffing progress report for any positions not filled onsite [CDRL A011]. 

1.2.3. Key Personnel: Key personnel shall be experienced in their respective functional areas and meet the minimum qualifications as detailed in Paragraph 5.0 of this PWS. (See applicable appendices for required specialized qualifications). Per paragraph 1.5.3. of the AFCAP V Basic contract, the Contractor shall designate in writing the Site Manager (SM) and Alternate Site Manager (ASM) and provide to the ACO and COR for acceptance NLT the first day of the transition period. The designated SM or ASM shall be on-site at all times to oversee all contract operations. The Contractor shall notify the ACO and COR of the departure of any individual in a key position within 24 hours of discovery and shall immediately identify their temporary substitution. Replacements for key positions shall be on site NLT two (2) weeks from notification to COR. The Contractor shall submit their plan for permanent placement within two (2) calendar days of key position vacancy with weekly updates until the permanent replacement arrives on site. 

1.2.3.1. Site Manager (SM): The SM is responsible for meeting Contractor’s work requirements, including scheduling of personnel, work supervision, and quality control for all tasks associated with this TO. The SM shall have full authority to act for the Contractor on all TO matters relating to daily operation of this TO. The SM shall be available within 30 minutes of notification to meet on the installation with Government personnel (designated by the ACO) to discuss focus areas. 

1.2.3.2. Alternate Site Manager (ASM): TheASM is designated by the Contractor to serve in an extended absence of the Site Manager. The ASM have full authority to act for the Contractor relating to daily contract operation in the absence of the SM. The ASM is responsible for meeting Contractor’s work requirements, including scheduling of personnel, work supervision, and quality control. The ASM shall be available within 30 minutes of notification to meet on the installation with Government personnel (designated by the ACO) to discuss focus areas. 

1.2.4. Monthly Status Reports (MSR): [CDRL A004]. MSRs shall be submitted to the COR and ACO beginning 30 calendar days after start of the Period of Performance (PoP). In addition to the content required for MSR, as detailed in the AFCAP V Basic Contract PWS, the Contractor shall complete and submit the required joint inventories and include them with the MSR when appropriate. IAW the AFCAP V Basic Contract Paragraph 4.10.1.3., the IPE report shall also be submitted. 

1.2.5 Task Order Deficiencies: Contractor shall acknowledge discrepancies and valid complaints within 24 hours, and shall achieve resolution within 48 hours. When performance deficiencies exist, the ACO, Functional Commander or designee, COR, other Government personnel as appropriate, and the Contractor shall meet weekly to discuss the Contractor’s performance. The following issues shall be discussed: opportunities to improve the contract, any modifications required of the contract, unsatisfactory inspections against each performance objective (PO) observed and steps taken by the Contractor to prevent occurrences in the future. The Contractor shall provide a summation of unsatisfactory inspections and provide insight into any identified trends [CDRL A007]. Should the Contractor not concur with the Government’s input to the minutes, the Contractor shall provide a written notification to the ACO and PCO identifying areas of non- concurrence for resolution. When no performance deficiencies exist, the ACO may limit the frequency of such meetings to correspond to current installation conditions. 

1.2.6. Recall Roster: The Contractor shall establish and maintain a recall roster for all contractor employees (including subcontractor employees) and within five (5) calendar days after CTORD provide the recall roster to the ACO and COR. The Contractor shall submit an updated recall roster within 24 hours of any changes. 

1.2.7. The Contractor is responsible to conduct all business and performance requirements associated with this TO IAW all U.S. and HN applicable laws, regulations, codes and standards. The Contractor shall report ALL contractor labor hours (including subcontractor labor hours) required for performance of services provided under this contract via a secure data collection site per AFCAP V Basic contract paragraph 1.3.8.16. The Contractor is required to completely fill in all required data fields at http://www.sam.gov. 

1.2.8. Quality Control Plan (QCP): Revisions to the TO specific QCP shall be submitted to the PCO, AFCAP Program Manager, ACO, and COR within 10 calendar days after start of the PoP. The Contractor’s quality inspection documentation (e.g., metrics and reports) shall be made available to the COR/ACO upon request. The reports shall provide information on the methods of inspection, what was inspected, discrepancies found and actions to correct and preclude recurrence [CDRL A014]. 

1.2.9. LN/OCN Labor Force: LN and OCN personnel are not allowed to be employed on this task order. 

1.2.10. The Government reserves the right to have the contractor provide data and information during performance of the task order via the following CDRLs: 

CDRL: A001 - AFCAP Management Plan   
CDRL: A002 - Task Order Management Plan (TOMP)   
CDRL: A003 - Travel/Trip Reports   
CDRL: A004 - Monthly Status Reports   
CDRL: A005 - Flash Reports   
CDRL: A006 - Monthly Performance and Cost Reports   
CDRL: A007 - Minutes of Meetings/Conferences   
CDRL: A008 - Engineering Drawings/Plans and As-Built Drawings   
CDRL: A009 - Presentation Materials   
CDRL: A010 - Color Photographs, Prints, Photo Documentation   
CDRL: A011 - Task Order Situation Reports   
CDRL: A012 - Verification of Key Personnel Security Clearances   
CDRL: A013 - Weekly Report of Deployed Task Order Personnel   
CDRL: A014 - Task Order Specific Quality Control Plan   
CDRL: A015 - Installation Specific Reporting   
CDRL: A016 - Government Furnished Property (GFP); Reporting of GFP 

1.2.10.1. The absence of any Contract Data Requirement List item from a specific PWS paragraph reference shall not detract from its enforceability nor limit the rights or remedies of the Government under any other provision of the contract. See Appendix E for CDRL details. 

2.0. SERVICE SUMMARY: Periodic inspections will be the monitoring method used for performance surveillance. Re-performance of unacceptable services at no additional cost will be the preferred course of action when appropriate. The absence of any contract requirement from the Service Summary shall not detract from its enforceability nor limit the rights or remedies of the Government under any other provision of the contract. The below Performance Thresholds identify the SATISFACTORY level of performance. 

3.1. Government Property (GP): Management: The Contractor shall adhere to all Government Property management processes, procedures, and reporting requirements as defined in FAR 45 and associated regulations, including CDRL A016, as outlined in the AFCAP V main contract. See Appendix A for property definitions. This contract contains Government Furnished Property (GFP) and/or CMGP, as listed in Appendix D. 

3.1.1. Government Furnished Property (GFP)/Contractor Managed Government Property (CMGP) Inventory Management Requirements: The purpose of these inventory requirements is to ensure accountability, maintain accurate records, and prevent loss or damage to GFP and/or CMGP. 

3.1.1.1. The Contractor and the COR shall perform $100 \%$ inventory inspections and periodic spot inspections of GFP/CMGP. The Contractor shall conduct all inventory inspections according to the schedule outlined below. The Contractor shall schedule all inventories and provide the necessary resources to complete inventories efficiently. Upon completion of inventories, the Contractor and COR shall jointly sign and date

to support Contractor vehicles for official use only. Official use includes those activities that directly support the requirements of this PWS. When contractors are lodged off-base due to lodging non-availability or mission requirements, the commute to and from the base is considered official use and will be provided for commutes up to $1 0 0 ~ \mathrm { k m }$ (each way). Fuel is not authorized for contractor's personal use. Fuel consumption will be calculated at a rate of $2 7 \mathrm { k m / g a l }$ for tracking purposes. For bases that have implemented the VILKey system at the on-base station, contractor personnel will be issued VILKeys to refuel contractor vehicles. Contractor owned/leased vehicles includes contractor employee's owned/leased vehicles utilized for Official Use. Contractor shall provide the ACO/COR a vehicle usage log for each vehicle at the completion of the month documenting distance traveled for Official Use. 

C.5.1. Within 10 calendar days after CTORD, the contractor shall provide: 

(a) list of vehicles that will be used during the performance of the contract period to include year, make, model, and license plate number 

(b) the estimated monthly fuel usage, and fuel tank capacity for each vehicle to the ACO and the COR. The contractor shall report changes to the contractor’s vehicle fleet (additions, removals, or substitutions) to the ACO/COR within 10 calendar days of the change. 

C.5.2. Contractor Vehicle Fuel Report: Not later than the third day of each month, the contractor shall provide actual fuel consumption for contractor-owned vehicles for the previous month to the ACO and COR. The reports shall include: 

(a) a list of vehicles that used fuel during the month to include year, make, model, fuel type and license plate number 

(b) the actual number of gallons per vehicle used during the month (c) the date fuel was received for each vehicle during the month 

C.5.3. The contractor shall provide fuel use “estimates” each quarter to the ACO/COR, to include the fuel tank capacity for each vehicle. If the contractor determines that additional fuel is required, the contractor shall provide: 

(a) the new estimated monthly fuel usage and fuel tank capacity for each vehicle   
requiring the additional fuel   
(b) the reason for the additional fuel 

C.5.4. The following are prohibited uses of government fuel: 

(a) use for purposes other than executing requirements of the contract (or government   
contracts)   
(b) use of fuel in excess of what is needed to execute the requirements of the contract (or   
government contracts)   
(c) resale, exchange, or transfer 

C.5.5. If the contractor knowingly engages in any prohibited use of fuel, the contractor is liable to the government for the value of any government fuel beyond contract requirements and may be subject to contract termination for cause.

Computers, Printers/Plotters, Ancillary Hardware and Software: The Government will provide, maintain, repair and upgrade as necessary all computers printers/plotters, ancillary hardware and software required to accomplish the requirements in this PWS, unless otherwise specified within this PWS. 

C.6.1. Printers/Plotters: The Government will share printer/plotter equipment and provide the printer/plotter paper to the Contractor for use in accomplishing the requirements in this PWS, unless otherwise specified within this PWS. The Government will provide and maintain all ink cartridges and all printer/plotter paper.

Deliverable: Task Order Management Plan (TOMP) 

Deliverable Comments: The plan shall demonstrate how the Contractor shall fulfill the specific requirements of the subsequent, individual task order and shall be updated with new information as it is obtained or becomes available and this proposed update must be approved by the Contracting Officer prior to implementation. The plan shall be incorporated as part of the task order. The plan shall include designation of an empowered on-site manager with the ability to make decisions and direct all sub-contractors on behalf of the prime contractor and all subcontracting services necessary to maintain a capability to perform under the task order. The 

TOMP shall address all requirements for operation at the site(s). The TOMP shall include all equipment, labor (to include leave schedules and how work shall be covered during absences) and materials necessary to perform the task order. The Contractor shall incorporate and comply with Air Force contingency standards (e.g., drinking water standards, airfield marking), unless explicitly changed in a specific task order. Host Nation requirements shall be incorporated into the TOMP. The Contractor shall develop a Schedule showing each activity, their relationships, duration, critical path, associated critical risks and planned mitigation, and estimated costs. The Schedule shall include: activation, subcontracting, transportation, mobilization/demobilization, logistical support, surveying, site work, site visits, critical path and any other significant activity. 

Record Keeping. The Contractor shall create and maintain written and electronic records for each task order, of all contracting actions, including, but not limited to, key assumptions, performance documentation, quality control, property control (e.g. inventory, current location, condition, disposition). The Contractor shall make these records available to the Government upon request.

Deliverable: Government Furnished Property (GFP); Reporting of GFP 

Deliverable Comments: 

Government Furnished Property – On specific task orders the Government may provide GFP. When a task order intends to provide GFP to a contractor, the task order PWS will include a list of GFP prior to task order award and the beginning of period of performance. The GFP Attachment lists GFP anticipated to be transferred to the contractor via the task order award. The contractor shall conduct the first inventory working from the GFP Attachment to derive the first Inventory of Record within the time limits prescribed by the task order. The Contractor shall conduct this inventory to account for $100 \%$ of the property on the GFP Attachment and shall conduct the inventory jointly with the COR. The Contractor shall submit the completed and jointly signed inventory (both Contractor and COR) to the PCO. The PCO will incorporate Inventories of Record into task orders, and may add or delete GFP, via task order modification. Contractors shall not initiate off-cycle inventories to add or remove GFP. The PCO will use scheduled Inventories of Record to capture GFP changes. 

Government Furnished Property Reporting – The Contractor shall report GFP to the Contracting Officer within the task order specific time limits and using the current version of Governmentfurnished forms. The Contractor shall report GFP: within 30 calendar days after the start of the period of performance, not later than 30 calendar days prior to the end of the period of performance, and annually. The Contractor shall base reports on physical inventories. The PCO may direct reports at other times to support changeover of CORs or for other Governmentdetermined reasons. The Government may grant the Contractor access to the GFP Module in the Wide Area Workflow (WAWF) application to enable the Contractor to consolidate all GFP reporting at a future date. The Contractor shall keep records of CMGP items in their Property 

Management System (PMS). The Contractor shall ensure accountability and provide operatorlevel maintenance to all non-GFP under the control of or being used by the Contractor in the performance of task orders. 

Date of First Submission: Thirty (30) calendar days after the start of the Period of Performance (PoP) 

Frequency: As required

The table structure consists of **4 columns**: - **Column 1**: Universal checkbox (□ unchecked in all rows; possibly for overall section approval). - **Column 2**: **CAAF Services** header (row 1), followed by a list of 10 potential services. - **Column 3**: Checkboxes associated with CAAF services (回 in row 1 and row 11; □ unchecked elsewhere; 回 may indicate a pre-filled or special status symbol). - **Column 4**: **NON-CAAF Services** header (row 1), followed by a list of 10 potential services. The table has **11 rows** total (1 header row + 10 service rows). Services are listed verbatim, with some referencing notes (Note #2 through #6), but note contents are **not provided** in the table. **No quantitative data** (e.g., numbers, rates, frequencies, hours, quantities, dollar amounts, coverage percentages) appears anywhere. All checkboxes are either □ (unchecked) or 回 (potentially checked/special; appears twice). The table implies binary selection (provide/does not provide) but provides no details on implementation. #### WORKLOAD DRIVERS No workload drivers are specified. - **Frequencies**: None (e.g., no "daily," "100 times per year," or similar). - **Quantities**: None (e.g., no personnel counts, meal volumes, or badge issuances). - **Hours**: None (e.g., no operating hours for DFAC or billeting). - **Coverage**: None (e.g., no population served or geographic scope). - **Service rates**: None (e.g., no cost per meal, per diem rates, or usage caps). The table only lists service *types* for selection; no drivers define volume or cadence. **CAAF Services Listed** (potential workload items): - WEAPON (Note #2) - BILLETING (Note #3) - CAC - LOCAL ACCESS BADGE - DINING FACILITY (DFAC) (Note #4) - GOV'T FURNISHED MEALS (Note #4) - APO/FPO/POSTAL SERVICES - DIPLOMATIC POST OFFICE (DPO) - PRIMARY CARE (Note #5) - OTHER (See Remarks Field) (Note #6) **NON-CAAF Services Listed** (potential workload items): - MILITARY ISSUED CLOTHING - MILITARY ISSUED EQUIPMENT - MWR FACILITIES - MILITARY EXCHANGE - COMMISSARY - MILITARY BANKING - EXCESS BAGGAGE - MILAIR - TRANSPORTATION (other than MILAIR) - NONE #### REQUIREMENTS No explicit requirements, specifications, standards, performance criteria, or modal verbs (e.g., shall, must, will) are stated. The table functions as a **selection matrix** rather than a prescriptive spec: - Services are nominal (e.g., "BILLETING" implies housing provision if checked, per Note #3; no details like "shall provide 100 sq ft per person"). - References to notes (e.g., Note #4 for DFAC and GOV'T FURNISHED MEALS) suggest requirements may be defined elsewhere, but none are included here. - Implied binary requirement: Check □ or 回 to authorize service access for the personnel category. - No standards (e.g., AR 190-13 for billeting, TB MED 530 for food service) or criteria detailed. #### PERFORMANCE METRICS None identified. - **KPIs**: None (e.g., no "% availability" or "meals served per day"). - **Thresholds/SLAs**: None (e.g., no "99% uptime for DFAC" or "badge issuance within 24 hours"). - **Measurement methods**: None (e.g., no surveys, logs, or audits specified). PROVISIONS: 

52.204-24 Representation Regarding Certain Telecommunications and Video Surveillance Services or Equipment (NOV 2021) 

The Offeror shall not complete the representation at paragraph (d)(1) of this provision if the Offeror has represented that it "does not provide covered telecommunications equipment or services as a part of its offered products or services to the Government in the performance of any contract, subcontract, or other contractual instrument" in paragraph (c)(1) in the provision at 52.204-26, Covered Telecommunications Equipment or Services—Representation, or in paragraph (v)(2)(i) of the provision at 52.212- 

Offeror Representations and Certifications-Commercial Products or Commercial Services. 

The Offeror shall not complete the representation in paragraph (d)(2) of this provision if the Offeror has represented that it "does not use covered telecommunications equipment or services, or any equipment, system, or service that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services" in paragraph (c)(2) of the provision at 52.204-26, or in paragraph (v)(2)(ii) of the provision at 52.212-3. 

(a) Definitions. As used in this provision— 

Backhaul, covered telecommunications equipment or services, critical technology, interconnection arrangements, reasonable inquiry, roaming, and substantial or essential component have the meanings provided in the clause 52.204-25, Prohibition on Contracting for Certain Telecommunications and Video Surveillance Services or Equipment. 

(b) Prohibition. 

(1) Section 889(a)(1)(A) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2019 (Pub. L. 115-232) prohibits the head of an executive agency on or after August 13, 2019, from procuring or obtaining, or extending or renewing a contract to procure or obtain, any equipment, system, or service that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services as a substantial or essential component of any system, or as critical technology as part of any system. Nothing in the prohibition shall be construed to— 

(i) Prohibit the head of an executive agency from procuring with an entity to provide a service that connects to the facilities of a third-party, such as backhaul, roaming, or interconnection arrangements; or 

(ii) Cover telecommunications equipment that cannot route or redirect user data traffic or cannot permit visibility into any user data or packets that such equipment transmits or otherwise handles. 

(2) Section 889(a)(1)(B) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2019 (Pub. L. 115-232) prohibits the head of an executive agency on or after August 13, 2020, from entering into a contract or extending or renewing a contract with an entity that uses any equipment, system, or service that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services as a substantial or essential component of any system, or as critical technology as part of any system. This prohibition applies to the use of covered telecommunications equipment or services, regardless of whether that use is in performance of work under a Federal contract. Nothing in the prohibition shall be construed to— 

(i) Prohibit the head of an executive agency from procuring with an entity to provide a service that connects to the facilities of a third-party, such as backhaul, roaming, or interconnection arrangements; or 

(ii) Cover telecommunications equipment that cannot route or redirect user data traffic or cannot permit visibility into any user data or packets that such equipment transmits or otherwise handles. 

(c) Procedures. The Offeror shall review the list of excluded parties in the System for Award Management (SAM) ( https://www.sam.gov) for entities excluded from receiving federal awards for "covered telecommunications equipment or services". 

(d) Representation. The Offeror represents that— 

(1) It □ will, □ will not provide covered telecommunications equipment or services to the Government in the performance of any contract, subcontract or other contractual instrument resulting from this solicitation. The Offeror shall provide the additional disclosure information required at paragraph (e)(1) of this section if the Offeror responds "will" in paragraph (d)(1) of this section; and 

(2) After conducting a reasonable inquiry, for purposes of this representation, the Offeror represents that— 

It □ does, □ does not use covered telecommunications equipment or services, or use any equipment, system, or service that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services. The Offeror shall provide the additional disclosure information required at paragraph (e)(2) of this section if the Offeror responds "does" in paragraph (d)(2) of this section. 

(e) Disclosures. 

(1) Disclosure for the representation in paragraph (d)(1) of this provision. If the Offeror has responded "will" in the representation in paragraph (d)(1) of this provision, the Offeror shall provide the following information as part of the offer: 

(i) For covered equipment— 

(A) The entity that produced the covered telecommunications equipment (include entity name, unique entity identifier, CAGE code, and whether the entity was the original equipment manufacturer (OEM) or a distributor, if known); 

(B) A description of all covered telecommunications equipment offered (include brand; model number, such as OEM number, manufacturer part number, or wholesaler number; and item description, as applicable); and 

(C) Explanation of the proposed use of covered telecommunications equipment and any factors relevant to determining if such use would be permissible under the prohibition in paragraph (b)(1) of this provision. 

(ii) For covered services— 

(A) If the service is related to item maintenance: A description of all covered telecommunications services offered (include on the item being maintained: Brand; model number, such as OEM number, manufacturer part number, or wholesaler number; and item description, as applicable); or 

(B) If not associated with maintenance, the Product Service Code (PSC) of the service being provided; and explanation of the proposed use of covered telecommunications services and any factors relevant to determining if such use would be permissible under the prohibition in paragraph (b)(1) of this provision. 

(2) Disclosure for the representation in paragraph (d)(2) of this provision. If the Offeror has responded "does" in the representation in paragraph (d)(2) of this provision, the Offeror shall provide the following information as part of the offer: 

(i) For covered equipment— 

(A) The entity that produced the covered telecommunications equipment (include entity name, unique entity identifier, CAGE code, and whether the entity was the OEM or a distributor, if known); 

(B) A description of all covered telecommunications equipment offered (include brand; model number, such as OEM number, manufacturer part number, or wholesaler number; and item description, as applicable); and 

(C) Explanation of the proposed use of covered telecommunications equipment and any factors relevant to determining if such use would be permissible under the prohibition in paragraph (b)(2) of this provision. 

(ii) For covered services— 

(A) If the service is related to item maintenance: A description of all covered telecommunications services offered (include on the item being maintained: Brand; model number, such as OEM number, manufacturer part number, or wholesaler number; and item description, as applicable); or 

(B) If not associated with maintenance, the PSC of the service being provided; and explanation of the proposed use of covered telecommunications services and any factors relevant to determining if such use would be permissible under the prohibition in paragraph (b)(2) of this provision. 

(End of provision)

52.209-11 Representation by Corporations Regarding Delinquent Tax Liability or a Felony Conviction under any Federal Law (Feb 2016) 

(a) As required by sections 744 and 745 of Division E of the Consolidated and Further Continuing Appropriations Act, 2015 (Pub. L. 113-235), and similar provisions, if contained in subsequent appropriations acts, the Government will not enter into a contract with any corporation that– 

(1) Has any unpaid Federal tax liability that has been assessed, for which all judicial and administrative remedies have been exhausted or have lapsed, and that is not being paid in a timely manner pursuant to an agreement with the authority responsible for collecting the tax liability, where the awarding agency is aware of the unpaid tax liability, unless an agency has considered suspension or debarment of the corporation and made a determination that suspension or debarment is not necessary to protect the interests of the Government; or 

(2) Was convicted of a felony criminal violation under any Federal law within the preceding 24 months, where the awarding agency is aware of the conviction, unless an agency has considered suspension or debarment of the corporation and made a determination that this action is not necessary to protect the interests of the Government. 

(b) The Offeror represents that– 

(1) It is ???? is not a corporation that has any unpaid Federal tax liability that has been assessed, for which all judicial and administrative remedies have been exhausted or have lapsed, and that is not being paid in a timely manner pursuant to an agreement with the authority responsible for collecting the tax liability; and 

(2) It is is not a corporation that was convicted of a felony criminal violation under a Federal law within the preceding 24 months. 

(End of provision)

52.217-8 -- Option to Extend Services (Nov 1999) 

The Government may require continued performance of any services within the limits and at the rates specified in the contract. These rates may be adjusted only as a result of revisions to prevailing labor rates provided by the Secretary of Labor. The option provision may be exercised more than once, but the total extension of performance hereunder shall not exceed 6 months. The Contracting Officer may exercise the option by written notice to the Contractor within 14 calendar days prior to contract expiration. 

52.217-9 -- Option to Extend the Term of the Contract (Mar 2000) 

(a) The Government may extend the term of this contract by written notice to the Contractor within 30 calendar days prior to contract expiration; provided that the Government gives the Contractor a preliminary written notice of its intent to extend at least 60 calendar days before the contract expires. The preliminary notice does not commit the Government to an extension. 

(b)If the Government exercises this option, the extended contract shall be considered to include this option clause. 

(c) The total duration of this contract, including the exercise of any options under this clause, shall not exceed 5 years. 

(End of clause) 

252.232-7006 Wide Area WorkFlow Payment Instructions (JAN 2023) 

(a) Definitions. As used in this clause— 

“Department of Defense Activity Address Code (DoDAAC)” is a six position code that uniquely identifies a unit, activity, or organization. 

“Document type” means the type of payment request or receiving report available for creation in Wide Area WorkFlow (WAWF). 

“Local processing office (LPO)” is the office responsible for payment certification when payment certification is done external to the entitlement system. 

“Payment request” and “receiving report” are defined in the clause at 252.232-7003 , Electronic Submission of Payment Requests and Receiving Reports. 

(b) Electronic invoicing. The WAWF system provides the method to electronically process vendor payment requests and receiving reports, as authorized by Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS) 252.232-7003 , Electronic Submission of Payment Requests and Receiving Reports. 

(c) WAWF access. To access WAWF, the Contractor shall— (1) Have a designated electronic business point of contact in the System for Award Management at https://www.sam.gov; and 

(2) Be registered to use WAWF at https://wawf.eb.mil/ following the step-by-step procedures for selfregistration available at this web site. 

(d) WAWF training. The Contractor should follow the training instructions of the WAWF Web-Based Training Course and use the Practice Training Site before submitting payment requests through WAWF. 

Both can be accessed by selecting the “Web Based Training” link on the WAWF home page at https://wawf.eb.mil/ 

(e) WAWF methods of document submission. Document submissions may be via web entry, Electronic Data Interchange, or File Transfer Protocol. 

(f) WAWF payment instructions. The Contractor shall use the following information when submitting payment requests and receiving reports in WAWF for this contract or task or delivery order: 

(1) Document type. The Contractor shall submit payment requests using the following document type(s): 

(i) For cost-type line items, including labor-hour or time-and-materials, submit a cost voucher. 

(ii) For fixed price line items— 

(A) That require shipment of a deliverable, submit the invoice and receiving report specified by the Contracting Officer. 

(B) For services that do not require shipment of a deliverable, submit either the Invoice 2in1, which meets the requirements for the invoice and receiving report, or the applicable invoice and receiving report, as specified by the Contracting Officer. 

(iii) For customary progress payments based on costs incurred, submit a progress payment request. 

(iv) For performance based payments, submit a performance based payment request. 

(v) For commercial item financing, submit a commercial item financing request. 

(2) Fast Pay requests are only permitted when Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 52.213-1 is included in the contract. 

(3) Document routing. The Contractor shall use the information in the Routing Data Table below only to fill in applicable fields in WAWF when creating payment requests and receiving reports in the system. 

Routing Data Table\* 

(4) Payment request. The Contractor shall ensure a payment request includes documentation appropriate to the type of payment request in accordance with the payment clause, contract financing clause, or Federal Acquisition Regulation 52.216-7, Allowable Cost and Payment, as applicable. 

(5) Receiving report. The Contractor shall ensure a receiving report meets the requirements of DFARS Appendix F. 

(g) WAWF point of contact. 

(1) The Contractor may obtain clarification regarding invoicing in WAWF from the following contracting activity’s WAWF point of contact. 

FA8051-26-R-1008 FOPR Attachment 3: Additional FAR Provisions and Clauses 31 December 2025 

(2) Contact the WAWF helpdesk at 866-618-5988, if assistance is needed. 

(End of clause)
