1.3.8.13; 1.3.8.13 subparagraphs 

Deliverable: Government Furnished Property (GFP); Reporting of GFP 

Deliverable Comments: 

Government Furnished Property – On specific task orders the Government may provide GFP. When a task order intends to provide GFP to a contractor, the task order PWS will include a list of GFP prior to task order award and the beginning of period of performance. The GFP Attachment lists GFP anticipated to be transferred to the contractor via the task order award. The contractor shall conduct the first inventory working from the GFP Attachment to derive the first Inventory of Record within the time limits prescribed by the task order. The Contractor shall conduct this inventory to account for $100 \%$ of the property on the GFP Attachment and shall conduct the inventory jointly with the COR. The Contractor shall submit the completed and jointly signed inventory (both Contractor and COR) to the PCO. The PCO will incorporate Inventories of Record into task orders, and may add or delete GFP, via task order modification. Contractors shall not initiate off-cycle inventories to add or remove GFP. The PCO will use scheduled Inventories of Record to capture GFP changes. 

Government Furnished Property Reporting – The Contractor shall report GFP to the Contracting Officer within the task order specific time limits and using the current version of Governmentfurnished forms. The Contractor shall report GFP: within 30 calendar days after the start of the period of performance, not later than 30 calendar days prior to the end of the period of performance, and annually. The Contractor shall base reports on physical inventories. The PCO may direct reports at other times to support changeover of CORs or for other Governmentdetermined reasons. The Government may grant the Contractor access to the GFP Module in the Wide Area Workflow (WAWF) application to enable the Contractor to consolidate all GFP reporting at a future date. The Contractor shall keep records of CMGP items in their Property 

Management System (PMS). The Contractor shall ensure accountability and provide operatorlevel maintenance to all non-GFP under the control of or being used by the Contractor in the performance of task orders. 

Date of First Submission: Thirty (30) calendar days after the start of the Period of Performance (PoP) 

Frequency: As required 

NOTE #1: CDRL A001 of the AFCAP Main Contract shall not be adjusted. 

NOTE #2: The AFCAP Main Contract CDRLs not identified in this PWS may still apply, e.g., Flash reports. 

NOTE #3: Delivery Table requirements may change at the request of the Contracting Officer. 

APPENDIX F 

TRANSIENT ALERT SERVICES (TAS) 

F.1. SCOPE. The Contractor shall perform TAS to include transient aircraft management control, arrival, processing and departure services IAW the requirements of this document. The Contractor shall perform all specific tasks prior to the scheduled departure time to meet transient aircraft arrival, processing and departure schedules unless otherwise directed by the aircraft commander or Maintenance Operations Center (MOC) for a faster turn. An aircraft with a Distinguished Visitor (DV) may have a higher priority than other aircraft. The Government will notify the Contractor of any increased emphasis required because of the DV mission. 

F.1.1. Unless specifically identified as not providing reciprocal services, all foreign countries are assumed to be under an agreement for the exchange of routine airport services or be providing similar services to the United States. Therefore, if requested, ALL FOREIGN MILITARY and FOREIGN STATE/COMMERCIAL AIRCRAFT should be provided these routine airport services without cost. Routine airport services include: use of runways and taxiways and air traffic services, aircraft parking, ground servicing to include "follow-me" vehicles, marshalling, wheel chocking, power carts, crew and passenger baggage on/off loading, screening, transportation to/from the aircraft, terminal, and base operations. Additional routine services may be provided on a non-reimbursable basis at the discretion of the U.S. wing commander, provided there is no additional cost to the Air Force to do so. 

F.1.2. REQUIREMENTS. The requirements described in this appendix are applicable to all sites covered in appendices G through L. 

F.1.3. GENERAL. The Contractor shall establish a transient aircraft management and control system and shall perform the following IAW the applicable T.O.(s). The Contractor shall perform specified “Transient Aircraft Management” services as listed in paragraphs F.1.3.1. – F.1.3.6 and paragraphs F.1.3.8.- F.1.3.21, with no more than a total of two (2) failure to perform tasks allowed for all paragraphs at any location, per month. Additional location specific requirements are detailed in appendices G through L. 

F.1.3.1. Prepare and document AF Form 861, Base/Transient Job Control Number Register, on all transient aircraft and provide the reports to the units specified in site specific appendix and provide AFCENT A4M an electronic copy monthly for each site IAW DAFI 21-101. Keep on file for the duration of the contract. Additionally, prepare a locally devised spreadsheet showing the number and type of each aircraft that required TAS. A sample spreadsheet will be provided to the contractor after task order award. 

F.1.3.2. Coordinate parking locations for transient aircraft parking outside transient ramp with site specific MOC and/or Airfield Management depending on where/what type of aircraft needs to be parked. Parking must be coordinated with the controlling agency prior to taking the aircraft to the parking area. 

F.1.3.3. Retrieve all drag chutes from the runway and taxi-ways when requested by Airfield Management. Turn in the chutes to the user organization. 

F.1.3.4. Coordinate with Petroleum, Oil & Lubricants (POL), for fuel delivery to include the prepositioning of fuel truck(s) as required. Coordinate with site specific POCs for Aircraft Ground Equipment (AGE) requirements and for fleet and passenger service as required. 

F.1.3.5. Coordinate maintenance or service requirements with the MOC or Expeditionary Maintenance Squadron/Flight (EMXS/EMXF) Production Superintendent/Supervisor as required by the site. 

F.1.3.5.1. When aircraft cannot fly due to technical data or parts, notify the site EMXS/EMXF Production Superintendent/Supervisor for specialist support and Airfield Management for parking spot coordination. If specialist or maintenance support does not exist within site Expeditionary Air Mobility Squadron (EAMS)/Expeditionary Air Squadron (EAS) or on base, the site location’s MOC will request and coordinate Maintenance Recovery Team (MRT) support. 

F.1.3.6. Notify Base Operations dispatch personnel when the aircraft is ready for the flight crew. 

F.1.3.7. Remove foreign object (FO) from transient aircraft parking ramp, work area(s) and parking location(s) outside transient parking ramp. Contact Airfield Management sweeper service as needed. Foreign Object Debris (FOD) program will meet or exceed all requirements in AFI 21- 101 and local guidance. As a minimum, Transient Alert will appoint a FOD Program Manager within the Transient Alert Section to assist with AFI 21-101 compliance and applicable base Operating Instructions (OI). Removal of FO must occur $100 \%$ of the time. 

F.1.3.8. Notify MOC of transient aircraft arrival to designated parking spot via Land Mobile Radio (LMR) or other communication device. Notify MOC when transient aircraft taxi out of designated parking spot via LMR or other communication device. Notify MOC of aircraft remaining over night. Ensure that aircraft status and parking location is verified with the MOC at the beginning and end of each shift. 

F.1.3.9. Maintain Air Force T.O. Library and Forms files applicable to transient maintenance IAW T.O. 00-5-1. The Air Force will provide T.O.s and/or checklists to the contractor. The Contractor shall sign for these items. If a T.O. is identified as missing, the Contractor is responsible for coordination to obtain a new one through the local T.O. Distribution Office, usually located within the Expeditionary Maintenance Group. As a minimum, the T.O. library will contain Launch, Recovery and Servicing T.O.’s for the primary aircraft transiting that location. T.O. 00-25-172 shall also be maintained. Aircraft Technical Order Libraries that travel on board with the aircraft (as is the case with C-5s, C-130s, etc. shall be sufficient to meet this requirement). Locally assigned units may also fulfill this role. 

F.1.3.10. Obtain and turn-in necessary parts for transient aircraft by contacting local supply customer service section in the Expeditionary Logistics Readiness Squadron (ELRS). The host AF wing will establish any supply accounts, as required. 

F.1.3.11. The SM, or his/her representative, will attend meetings, briefings and participate in exercises involving transient aircraft as requested by EMXS/EMXF Production Superintendent through the COR and ACO. 

F.1.3.12. Ensure compliance with hazardous material handling and material safety data sheet programs IAW DAFI 90-821 and all local procedures. When established by EMXS/EMXF, ensure the hazardous waste accumulation point is emptied every 24 hours if full and transported to the Air Expeditionary Wing (AEW) Hazardous Waste disposal site. 

F.1.3.13. Monitor all transient aircraft ramp activities at all times. 

F.1.3.14. Dispatch personnel to drive and operate the mobile stair case unit as requested. Coordinate through MOC at locations where this activity is covered by air terminal operations center (ATOC) personnel. 

F.1.3.15. Ensure an effective tool control program is established IAW with procedures specified in DAFI 21-101, and Expeditionary Maintenance Group (EMXG) OI to include lost tool procedures. 

F.1.3.16. Notify Airfield Management and wing safety office immediately when evidence of a bird strike is found. Provide flight commander(s) an AEW/SE bird strike worksheet during debriefing when these incidents occur and forward to the COR, EMXG/QA and Wing Chief of Safety on the day of occurrence or next duty day when safety personnel are available. 

F.1.3.17. Implement and monitor a customer feedback program to measure customer satisfaction and take prompt corrective action on adverse trends. 

F.1.3.18. Submit appointment letters to the Maintenance Flight Chief and the COR designating primary and alternate Facility Managers, HAZMAT Monitors, Equipment Custodians, Technical Order Monitors, Composite Tool Kit Monitors, Joint Oil Analysis Program (JOAP) Monitors, LMR Custodians, Communication Security (COMSEC) Officers, Vehicle Control Officer, FOD Program Manager and Test, Measurement, & Diagnostic Equipment (TMDE) Monitors. The letter is due by TO PoP start date and shall be kept current throughout the life of the contract. Changes shall be coordinated through the COR. 

F.1.3.19. Submit a report describing number of transient aircraft arrivals, departures, DVs, customer surveys, and other reportable events of the preceding month in a standardized format across all locations for the data that is submitted. This report is due electronically by the third work day of each month and to the Maintenance Flight Chief, AFCENT A4M, and COR. At locations where no Maintenance Flight Chief exists, forward copy to next higher authority. 

F.1.3.20. Train other TO employees on specific aircraft when required, and if not provided by the Government per PWS paragraph F.2. If Contractor hires an employee from the previous contractor and has already had the training, the Government will not retrain that employee just because the contractor changed. 

F.1.3.21. React to surge requirements on an as needed basis. Surge requirements are any above normal or average airflow into a TA location. The Government will provide surge requirements to the contractor as soon as possible so the contractor can make a determination if additional personnel are required. 

F.1.4. ARRIVAL SERVICES. The Contractor shall provide the following arrival services IAW DAFI 21-101, Maintenance Management of Aircraft, and Air Mobility Command (AMC) 21-101 Supplement and Air Combat Command (ACC) Sup I 21-101. At each location, the Contractor shall perform specified arrival services and tasks as listed in paragraphs F.1.4.1. – F.1.4.14. prior to the scheduled departure time unless otherwise directed by the aircraft commander or MOC for a faster turn, with no more than a total of three (3) failures to perform services allowed for all paragraphs at any location, per month. 

F.1.4.1. All transient aircraft must be recovered by Contractor immediately upon landing. As time permits, perform all other recovery services and prepare the aircraft to depart prior to the scheduled departure time unless otherwise directed by the aircraft commander or MOC for a faster turn. 

F.1.4.2. Meet transient aircraft upon arrival in the "Follow-Me" vehicle at the designated taxiway and lead into the assigned parking spot if requested by the control tower or Airfield Management. The Aircraft Marshaller shall provide signals to park and shut down all transient aircraft. “FollowMe” services shall be provided to any aircraft when requested by the control tower or Airfield Management. When operating along runways or taxi-ways, personnel shall maintain radio contact with control tower. LMR will be provided as GFE. The Contractor is responsible for maintaining control of LMRs. Maintenance on the radios will be at government expense. LMRs damaged due to negligence shall be at Contractor’s expense. 

F.1.4.3. Operate support equipment, fire extinguishers, chocks, boarding ramps, ground wires, powered and non-powered AGE and mobile staircase as required for aircraft arrival and departure. 

F.1.4.4. Check for hot brakes and report suspected hot brakes to the MOC. 

F.1.4.5. For F-16/U-2 mission design series transient aircraft, immediately report to the MOC if aircraft is suspected of a hydrazine leak. 

F.1.4.6. Install nose and/or main landing gear locks and pins, as required/requested by aircraft commanders. 

F.1.4.7. Install egress and safing pins as required. The Contractor shall not install aircraft explosive egress system safing pins. However, the Contractor shall coordinate with pilot(s) to check that status safety pins are installed on explosive egress system(s) prior to departing the aircraft. The Contractor shall also perform aircraft safe for maintenance procedures as required IAW tech data. Transient aircraft personnel not qualified on a particular aircraft shall notify the MOC. Note that a variety of aircraft are subject to arrive at each location as a divert; see Appendices G-L for estimated number and type of aircraft at each location. TA personnel shall be thoroughly familiar with recovering, servicing, and launching these aircraft. 

F.1.4.8. Insert wheel chocks before the aircraft commander departs the cockpit. 

F.1.4.9. Statically ground all aircraft if grounding points are available. 

F.1.4.10. Debrief the aircraft commander to determine aircraft status and service requirements. Document requirements in the applicable forms IAW T.O. 00-20-1 and local quality assurance 

(QA) approved checklist. Contractor shall build the debrief sheet in coordination with all sites.   
CORs will coordinate with the Administrative Contracting Officer (ACO) to approve. 

F.1.4.11. Tow, position, connect and operate aerospace ground and support equipment as required IAW applicable technical data. 

F.1.4.12. Install all aircraft Dash-21 as required. Inspect for and remove foreign object(s) prior to installing covers. 

F.1.4.13. All distinguished visitors with codes one (1) thru seven (7) will be parked in front of the DV lounge. When more than one coded aircraft arrives, the highest code will be parked closest to DV lounge in coordination with Airfield Management. 

F.1.4.14. Contact MOC for Weapons Crew personnel for safing transient aircraft with munitions emergencies. In the event of an unsafe munitions condition, immediately coordinate through the MOC for activation of Emergency Action Checklist. All TA personnel shall review annually and maintain current copies of the EMXG Emergency Action Check sheets within the control section and all flight line vehicles. 

F.1.5. PROCESSING TURN-AROUND SERVICES. The Contractor shall perform processing turn-around services on all transient aircraft as requested by the aircraft commander IAW DAFI 21-101, Maintenance Management of Aircraft, AMC 21-101 Supplement and Air Combat Command (ACC) Sup I 21-101. The Contractor shall initiate proper documentation for reimbursement IAW applicable directives. The Contractor shall perform specified “processing services” as listed in paragraphs F.1.5.1 – F.1.5.13; with no more than a total of two (2) failures to perform tasks allowed for all paragraphs at any location, per month. 

F.1.5.1. Check and service aircraft engine oil as required. Extract oil samples, complete DD Form 2026, and deliver to the Non-Destructive Inspection (NDI) laboratory within 45 minutes after aircraft shutdown. 

F.1.5.2. Refuel aircraft IAW MDS T.O. The Contractor is responsible for assisting the aircrew/pilots during refuel operations. This includes positioning/chocking/grounding the fuel truck, connecting fuel hose/handling over-the-wing hose, performing fire guard duties, and assisting fuel truck driver. Stow servicing equipment when refuel operations have been completed. Contractor will assist aircrew or MRT with defueling aircraft in the same manner, as required. Fuel spills shall be reported immediately to MOC. Utilize fuel spill response kits as required. Immediately contain and clean up spills IAW local directives. 

F.1.5.3. Operate, check and service liquid or gaseous oxygen, nitrogen, hydraulics, fuels and oil systems as required IAW MDS T.O. and document aircraft 781 series forms IAW T.O. 00-20-1. The Contractor shall provide assistance if required when a Flying Crew Chief (FCC) is on-board accomplishing these requirements. 

F.1.5.4. Perform a general walk-around inspection of the aircraft to ensure flight worthiness. If an FCC person is on-board accomplishing these requirements, the Contractor shall provide assistance if required. Notify the aircraft commander/flight crew as soon as possible concerning any discrepancies noted. If needed, coordinate with either MOC or EMXS/EMXF Production Superintendent/Supervisor for specialist support. 

F.1.5.5. After aircraft walk-around inspection, enter and clear discrepancy entries in AFTO Form 781 series forms IAW 00-20 series T.O.s and EMXG/CC guidance. Refer to T.O. 00-20-1, chapter 4, for further guidance. 

F.1.5.6. Secure/bed-down aircraft that remain overnight. 

F.1.5.7. Support and assist maintenance specialist or MRTs as directed by the EMXS/EMXF Production Superintendent/Supervisor.

F.1.5. PROCESSING TURN-AROUND SERVICES. The Contractor shall perform processing turn-around services on all transient aircraft as requested by the aircraft commander IAW DAFI 21-101, Maintenance Management of Aircraft, AMC 21-101 Supplement and Air Combat Command (ACC) Sup I 21-101. The Contractor shall initiate proper documentation for reimbursement IAW applicable directives. The Contractor shall perform specified “processing services” as listed in paragraphs F.1.5.1 – F.1.5.13; with no more than a total of two (2) failures to perform tasks allowed for all paragraphs at any location, per month. 

F.1.5.1. Check and service aircraft engine oil as required. Extract oil samples, complete DD Form 2026, and deliver to the Non-Destructive Inspection (NDI) laboratory within 45 minutes after aircraft shutdown. 

F.1.5.2. Refuel aircraft IAW MDS T.O. The Contractor is responsible for assisting the aircrew/pilots during refuel operations. This includes positioning/chocking/grounding the fuel truck, connecting fuel hose/handling over-the-wing hose, performing fire guard duties, and assisting fuel truck driver. Stow servicing equipment when refuel operations have been completed. Contractor will assist aircrew or MRT with defueling aircraft in the same manner, as required. Fuel spills shall be reported immediately to MOC. Utilize fuel spill response kits as required. Immediately contain and clean up spills IAW local directives. 

F.1.5.3. Operate, check and service liquid or gaseous oxygen, nitrogen, hydraulics, fuels and oil systems as required IAW MDS T.O. and document aircraft 781 series forms IAW T.O. 00-20-1. The Contractor shall provide assistance if required when a Flying Crew Chief (FCC) is on-board accomplishing these requirements. 

F.1.5.4. Perform a general walk-around inspection of the aircraft to ensure flight worthiness. If an FCC person is on-board accomplishing these requirements, the Contractor shall provide assistance if required. Notify the aircraft commander/flight crew as soon as possible concerning any discrepancies noted. If needed, coordinate with either MOC or EMXS/EMXF Production Superintendent/Supervisor for specialist support. 

F.1.5.5. After aircraft walk-around inspection, enter and clear discrepancy entries in AFTO Form 781 series forms IAW 00-20 series T.O.s and EMXG/CC guidance. Refer to T.O. 00-20-1, chapter 4, for further guidance. 

F.1.5.6. Secure/bed-down aircraft that remain overnight. 

F.1.5.7. Support and assist maintenance specialist or MRTs as directed by the EMXS/EMXF Production Superintendent/Supervisor. 

F.1.5.8. Check tires and struts for condition as required. Document 781 series forms as required IAW MDS T.O. 00-20 series TOs and local EMXG directives. 

F.1.5.9. Check canopy(s) and windscreens for condition. If suspect, call the EMXS/EMXF Production Superintendent/Supervisor and document 781 series forms IAW 00-20 series T.O.s and directives. Clean as required or as requested by the aircrew. 

F.1.5.10. Sign off AF Form 1800 (Operator’s Inspection Guide and Trouble Report) on all vehicles daily (when used). If not used every day, sign off every at least every seven Calendar days. Perform the following service(s) on vehicles and equipment weekly: cleaning, washing, check fluid levels and replenish, if required; painting and stenciling chocks; removing and installing deteriorated ropes on aircraft chocks; repair, removing and replacing ground wire; and preventive maintenance actions on related items. 

F.1.5.11. Contractor personnel shall accomplish the following minor maintenance and inspection(s) as required: 

F.1.5.11.1. Airframe systems: Open and/or close, remove and/or install airframe access panels, as required to check servicing and perform a general walk-around inspection of the aircraft. 

F.1.5.11.2. Aerospace Ground Equipment: Perform pre-use inspections IAW applicable guidance. 

F.1.5.12. Perform pre-use inspections on special purpose vehicles and document inspection compliance on applicable form. 

F.1.5.13. Operate tow vehicles and tow aircraft as required. Contractor shall ensure qualified personnel are available $2 4 / 7$ to tow aircraft. Coordinate tow approval through the EMXG/MOC or Airfield Management. The Contractor, with assistance of the aircrew, shall tow the aircraft as required. Some locations have deployed active duty (AD) units for certain Air Force MDS aircraft that may assist the Contractor, but that does not remove responsibility from the Contractor in accomplishing these requirements. 

F.1.6. DEPARTURE SERVICES. The Contractor shall perform departure services IAW applicable directives and as listed in paragraphs F.1.6.1. – F.1.6.3., with no more than a total of two (2) failure to perform tasks allowed for all paragaraphs at any location, per month. 

F.1.6.1. Brief the aircraft commander on the condition of the aircraft. The briefing should include at least a summary of maintenance and servicing performed, fuel load, and status of the aircraft. 

F.1.6.2. Launch aircraft IAW applicable checklist to include marshalling the aircraft out of the parking spot. 

F.1.6.3. Provide “Follow-Me” service as required. 

F.1.7. EMERGENCY SERVICES. The Contractor shall provide transient maintenance services during emergencies as required. Emergency services are defined as an unforeseen occurrence, a sudden and urgent occasion(s) for action. Examples are: weather divert(s), in flight emergencies (IFE), medical evacuation(s), emergency evacuation of aircraft due to fire, bomb threat, high winds or hurricane, earthquake, major fuel spill, hydrazine leakage, nuclear incident, aircraft crashes, hijacking, performance of services during crisis declared by National Command Authority or other contingency. Contractor shall assist with crash recovery as directed. Specialized Crash Recovery training is required for team members (e.g., handling composite materials and respirator training). Contractor personnel would be utilized in efforts to restore runway to operational status only. EMXG units are required by DAFI 21-101 to establish Crash Recovery programs for their airfield. Team chiefs will administer familiarization training to contractor personnel. Contractor personnel will only be augmentees. 

F.1.8. ADDITIONAL WORKLOAD REQUIREMENTS. The Contractor shall provide a list of stand-by personnel and telephone numbers to the COR and Airfield Manager for servicing transient aircraft during periods of potentially increased activity. A maximum response time of 75 minutes is required for personnel to report for duty. 

F.2. GOVERNMENT PROVIDED TRAINING. 

F.2.1. Crash recovery familiarization training semi-annually. Training will be conducted on site and arranged through the contractor Training Program Manager. Contractor personnel will serve in an augmentee position only in order to restore the runway to normal operations. When normal operations occur the contractor personnel shall return to their normal duties. 

F.2.2. Certificate of Competency, AF Form 483, for all government vehicles and special purpose vehicles for contractor employees. Training will be accomplished on-site. The contractor shall be responsible for requesting driver training for employees. Contractor personnel shall possess a valid state driver’s license. 

F.2.3. Initial training on aerospace ground and support equipment for two (2) Contractor employees. Training will be accomplished on-site. AGE familiarization training will be provided by the Government through the EMXS/EMXF AGE Flight/Section. Coordinate all training requests with AGE Flight/Section Chief. The Government will train one (1) Contractor representative thereafter on any modification and/or new equipment as required. 

F.2.4. Annual fire extinguisher training. Training will be accomplished on-site and arranged through the contractor Training Program Manager. 

F.2.5. Annual hazardous waste management training will be provided to the contractor’s primary and alternate managers. Training will be accomplished on-site and arranged through the Contractor’s Training Program Manager. 

F.2.6. Initial training on A-10, F-15, F-16, and F-18 inlet inspections as applicable, for contractor employees, possibly at an off-site location. Such training will be government-funded to include MIL-AIR transportation, meals, and on base lodging. 

F.2.7. The Government will provide limited training for KC-135, KC-10, C-20, C-5, C-17 and C40 aircraft. 

F.2.8. The Government will assist the Contractor in maintaining MDS specific certification levels throughout the length of the contract period of performance. The government will furnish applicable MDS specific training through the use of onsite resources, mobile training teams and transport to MDS specific locations. The Government will initially train two (2) Contractor personnel MDS specific tasks. Those technicians will, in turn, train other contractors. All training will be tracked electronically on a self generated form. This form will be provided to the COR monthly on the first day of the start of the performance assessment reporting period. 

F.2.9. The Contractor’s Training Program Manager shall coordinate for any training or familiarization training for new personnel required at an off-site location. This coordination will be through the COR to the EMXG. Training is a crucial link in the success of the mission and all TAS members should be fully qualified as quickly as possible to prevent mission impairment, especially in the case of divert aircraft (specified in each site annex). 

F.3. GOVERNMENT FURNISHED TECHNICAL ORDERS, PUBLICATIONS AND FORMS. 

F.3.1. The Contractor shall maintain a current Technical Order Distribution Account (TODA) and transient aircraft technical order library through Technical Order Distribution Office (TODO), for technical order requirements applicable to transient aircraft. The TODO may not be located on the installation; however, the contractor will have access to the closest TODO. T.O.s will be maintained, posted and up to date IAW DAFI 21-101,Aircraft and Equipment Maintenance Management and T.O. 00-5-1 AF Technical Order System. At locations with EMXS/EMXF units, the support section personnel will perform this role. All technical orders will be checked out as necessary from the support section. Aircraft Technical Order Libraries that travel on board with the aircraft (as is the case with C-5s, C-130s, etc…) will be sufficient to meet this requirement. 

F.3.2. The contractor shall maintain a current publications library and forms account and filing system with Publication Distribution Office (PDO) for Air Force publications and forms requirements applicable to transient aircraft. 

F.3.3. Upon completion of the contract, the contractor shall return to the government all issued technical orders, publications and forms. 

F.4. SUBMITTALS. 

F.4.1. FORM SUBMITTAL. Submit the following forms no later than the third work day after the end of the month: 

F.4.1.1. AF Form 861, Base/Transient Job Control Number Register. The Contractor shall notify the COR when completed and on file and maintain completed forms IAW applicable directives. All sites will provide electronic copies of a government provided spreadsheet detailing daily aircraft flow and type. The spreadsheet will also be sent to a4.a4mmshaw@afcent.af.mil. 

F.4.1.2. AF Form 1806, Operator's Inspection Guide and Trouble Report (Aircraft Towing, BaseMaintenance and High Reach). Contractor shall maintain one copy and send one copy to ELRS. 

F.4.2. SUBMITTAL TIMING. Submit a Bird Strike, Dropped Object, or Aircraft Mishap/incident Worksheet to the COR, QA and local Chief of Safety on the day of occurrence or next duty day when safety personnel are available. 

F.5. PERSONNEL TRAINING. 

F.5.1. The Contractor shall train, qualify and certify personnel for specific job tasks to be performed in transient maintenance services except those previously identified as being provided by the Government. Records of training, qualifications and certification shall be maintained by the contractor on file and made available for government review upon request. The Contractor shall use AF Form 797 for this purpose. 

F.5.2. The Contractor shall assign one (1) contract personnel to act as the site training manager, ensuring that all employees’ training records are current, and have all available training required to ensure mission success. 

F.6. SECURITY 

F.6.1. Security Training. All Contractor personnel will receive initial and recurring security education training coordinated through the COR. 

F.6.2. Listing of Personnel. The Contractor shall maintain a current listing of personnel. The list shall include personnel’s name, and level of security clearance. The list shall be provided to the CO and COR. An updated listing shall be provided when personnel status or information changes. 

F.6.3. AF Form 1199, USAF Restricted Area Badge. An AF Form 2586, Unescorted Entry Authorization Certificate, must be signed by appropriate government agencies before a Restricted Area Badge will be issued. 

F.6.4. Recovering Identification Media. The Contractor shall recover all government issued identification media from personnel who permanently depart the AOR for any reason before the contract expires; e.g. terminated for cause, retirement, etc. and turn into the AFCAP PMO, Tyndall AFB. 

F.6.5. For Official Use Only (FOUO). The Contractor shall comply with DODM5400.07_AFMAN 33-302, Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) Program requirements. These regulations set policy and procedures for the disclosure of records to the public and for marking, handling, transmitting and safeguarding FOUO material. 

F.6.6. Access to Restricted Areas: The Contractor shall not enter any restricted area without proper authorization from base security forces personnel or specified security managers. When Contractor access to restricted areas is required, the Contractor shall accomplish line badge application and processing per each base’s specified security procedures. Restricted area badges (line badges) will be required for some Contractor personnel. The Contractor shall complete obtaining badges and licenses prior to the first day of CTORD. 

F.6.7. AF Form 1199, USAF Restricted Area Badge. An AF Form 2586, Unescorted Entry Authorization Certificate, must be signed by appropriate government agencies before a Restricted Area Badge will be issued. 

F.6.8. Key Control: The Contractor shall maintain control of all keys issued to the Contractor. The Contractor shall follow AFMAN 34-135 and the base key control logs maintained on a share drive or in the facility key box. The Contractor shall maintain strict key controls at all times to prevent theft or safety/security issues. 

APPENDIX G 

AL UDEID AIR BASE (AUAB), QATAR 

G.1.0. These site-specific requirements supplement, and have precedence over, the remaining requirements of this PWS. 

G.1.1. The 379 EMXS may also direct the Contractor for a faster turn. When the number of aircraft requiring service precludes servicing at the same time, the Contractor shall perform service in the priority established by the 379 EMXS Maintenance Operations Center as applicable. 

G.1.2. The Contractor shall report all transient parking issues and tower notification issues to both the Airfield Manager and COR. 

G.1.3. Control of parking areas is detailed in AUABI 13-204. 

G.1.4. In order to maximize efficiency and safety on the AUAB main parking ramp and maintain continuity for DV protocol, the Contractor shall provide follow me service, and marshal aircraft in and out of the DV parking spots for all locally assigned DV coded aircraft arriving or departing AUAB. All maintenance and ground powered equipment operation will be the responsibility of the base assigned unit. Powered AGE requirement will be coordinated through AGE Dispatch by the owning unit prior to aircraft arriving on the main ramp. (Reference PWS paragraph F.1.3.2) 

G.1.5. Contractor’s personnel shall be qualified to tow all types of aircraft including: C-5, C-9, C12, ,C-20, C-21, C-32, C-37, C-40, C-130, SW-4, F-16, F-15, F/A 18, U-2, RQ-4, A-10, KC-10, EA-6B, B-747, B-757, B-767, MD-11, KC-135, AN-12, AN-124, and IL-76 etc. The Contractor shall coordinate tow approval through either the 379 MOC or 8 EAMS (or equivalent EAS), depending on location of the final parking spot. The Contractor shall document tow operators’ qualification on a special certification roster furnished by the contractor. NOTE. 8 EAMS (or equivalent EAS) will cover ground handling operations for unit assigned C-17s at AUAB. The contractor will cover ground handling for all C-17s transient to AUAB. (Reference PWS paragraph F.1.5) 

G.1.6. Due to the high volume of transient aircraft at AUAB and criticality of supporting the ATO, additional management actions are required to ensure support of diverted KC-135 aircraft from other locations. The majority of diverted KC-135 aircraft from other locations will depart AUAB as an Air Tasking Order (ATO) sortie. (Reference PWS paragraph F.1.5) 

G.1.6.1. To ensure adequate and timely support for these aircraft, the $3 4 0 ^ { \mathrm { t h } }$ AMU will have responsibility for supporting KC-135 aircraft that divert into AUAB, to include all aspects of readying them for flight (servicing, through/post/pre-flight inspections, and unscheduled maintenance). (Reference PWS paragraph F.1.5) 

G.1.6.2. Upon notification of inbound diverted KC-135 aircraft, MOC will notify both 379 EMXS Production Supervision and 379 EAMXS supervision who will then ensure the $3 4 0 ^ { \mathrm { t h } }$ AMU is prepared to support. The Contractor shall provide follow-me service as required. (Reference PWS paragraph F.1.5) 

G.1.7. The Contractor shall be responsible for operation of any requested powered AGE, (power units, air conditioners, etc.), for base assigned C-21 aircraft on DV outbound missions. Any unscheduled maintenance or servicing requirements remain the responsibility of the maintenance personnel assigned to those aircrafts. (Reference PWS paragraph F.1.5) 

G.1.8. The Contractor shall remove FO in the sun shade parking area at all times and all other areas where transient aircraft are currently parked. (Reference PWS paragraph F.1.3.7.) 

G.1.9. In the event of a ground emergency, the Contractor shall immediately evacuate the area IAW AlUAB ABI 11-250, Airfield Operations and Base Flying Procedures, until ground emergency vicinity is determined safe by the On Scene Commander

.2.10. The Government reserves the right to have the contractor provide data and information during performance of the task order via the following CDRLs:

1.2.10.1. The absence of any Contract Data Requirement List item from a specific PWS paragraph reference shall not detract from its enforceability nor limit the rights or remedies of the Government under any other provision of the contract. See Appendix E for CDRL details. 

2.0. SERVICE SUMMARY: Periodic inspections will be the monitoring method used for performance surveillance. Re-performance of unacceptable services at no additional cost will be the preferred course of action when appropriate. The absence of any contract requirement from the Service Summary shall not detract from its enforceability nor limit the rights or remedies of the Government under any other provision of the contract. The below Performance Thresholds identify the SATISFACTORY level of performance. 

3.1. Government Property (GP): Management: The Contractor shall adhere to all Government Property management processes, procedures, and reporting requirements as defined in FAR 45 and associated regulations, including CDRL A016, as outlined in the AFCAP V main contract. See Appendix A for property definitions. This contract contains Government Furnished Property (GFP) and/or CMGP, as listed in Appendix D. 

3.1.1. Government Furnished Property (GFP)/Contractor Managed Government Property (CMGP) Inventory Management Requirements: The purpose of these inventory requirements is to ensure accountability, maintain accurate records, and prevent loss or damage to GFP and/or CMGP. 

3.1.1.1. The Contractor and the COR shall perform $100 \%$ inventory inspections and periodic spot inspections of GFP/CMGP. The Contractor shall conduct all inventory inspections according to the schedule outlined below. The Contractor shall schedule all inventories and provide the necessary resources to complete inventories efficiently. Upon completion of inventories, the Contractor and COR shall jointly sign and date the inventory record. The inventory record must include a detailed listing of all GFP/CMGP, including quantity, condition, and identification numbers (e.g., serial numbers, asset tags). The Contractor shall submit a copy of jointly signed and dated inventory records to the PCO/ACO within five (5) calendar days of completed inspection. Inventory inspections shall occur at the following intervals: 

3.1.1.1.1. Initial Inspection: The Contractor shall conduct a joint $100 \%$ physical inventory of all GFP/CMGP with the COR NLT 30 calendar days after CTORD. 

3.1.1.1.2. Annual Inventory: The Contractor shall conduct a $100 \%$ physical inventory of all GFP/CMGP annually. The Contractor shall complete the annual inventory within 10 calendar days of the anniversary of the CTORD. 

3.1.1.1.3. Spot Inspections: The Contractor and the COR shall conduct spot inspections of at least $10 \%$ of the available inventory of all GFP/CMGP at least every six (6) months. Additional spot inspections may occur at the request of the COR. 

3.1.1.1.4. Final Inspection: The Contractor and the Government shall conduct a joint $100 \%$ physical inventory of all GFP/CMGP within 30 calendar days prior to the TO performance termination date. The TO cannot be closed until all inventory discrepancies are resolved. 

3.1.1.2. The Contractor conducting internal inventories shall annotate the location of the equipment and record property tag numbers or serial numbers. The Contractor shall provide awareness of Contractor internal inventories to the COR. The Contractor shall provide inventory results to the COR and key replacement personnel. The Contractor shall concurrently inspect property during inventories for correct marking. The Contractor shall assist in conducting local, non-reportable inventories when the COR’s change out. The Contractor shall immediately report missing items and excess items to the Government. 

3.1.1.3. Inventory Concurrent Actions: The Contractor shall concurrently inspect property during inventories for correct marking and shall ensure that all GFP/CMGP under the Contractor’s possession or control exhibits U.S. Government property markings. The Contractor shall tag corporate property as Contractor-owned property. The Contractor shall record property tag numbers, serial numbers, and similar identification information on inventories. The Contractor shall place a “PT” prefix to all property tag numbers when recording U.S. Government property tag numbers. The Contractor shall record property tag numbers, serial numbers, and similar identification such as markings or inscriptions on the Contractor’s PMS per line item. 

3.1.1.4. GFP and CMGP Labeling and Government-Conducted Inventory Records: The Contractor shall ensure that all property under their control, and all Contractor-owned property is labeled, marked, or tagged or similarly identified and that identification numbers from tags, labels, sticker, manufacturer’s serial numbers, data plates, etchings, or other similar labeling methods, are accurately recorded in the Contractor’s PMS. The Contractor shall not create duplicative entries of unique items in the Contractor’s PMS. The Contractor shall not formally inventory Government-managed/locally controlled Government property. The Contractor shall assist the Government in identifying, researching, and labelling Government property under the Contractor’s stewardship. Example: Base Equipment Control Office (BECO), Information Technology Equipment Custodian (ITEC) items, and communications end items which are periodically inventoried by Government personnel and have been locally sub-custodied to the Contractor are not required to be listed on the Contractor’s PMS. The Contractor shall maintain a copy of locally sub-custodied and inventoried by the Government inventories once completed and certified by the Government. The Contractor may develop and employ daily executable, local Contractor’s checklists of Government-controlled, Government-managed, Government-inventoried property in use by the contractor as a Best Management Practice (BMP). The Contractor shall provide all labeling, marking, tagging supplies and equipment. 

3.1.1.5. Inventory Records Retention: The Contractor shall electronically save and retain on file all completed inventory records for the life of the TO. 

3.1.2. Property Control Plan (PCP): The Contractor shall provide an updated TO-specific PCP to the ACO and AFCAP Program Management Office NLT 30 calendar days after the TO award date [CDRL A001]. The PCP shall identify in writing the Contractor’s manager, by position title, responsible for Government property management located on-site. The Contractor shall identify changes of key employees responsible for property management to the ACO in writing within 48 hours of each change and shall include this change in their next MSR [CDRL A004]. The Contractor shall comply with FAR Part 45 and FAR Clause 52.245-1 through -2 as applicable, when in possession of, receipt of, control of, or administratively managing Government property. 

3.1.2.1. The Contractor’s updated PCP shall address the management of operating supplies to include expendable items and consumable items. The Contractor shall manage expendable and consumable items as GFM, not personal property. Expendable items have a service life of less than two years and are operating supplies. Consumable items such as paper products, cleaning supplies, office supplies, fast-moving petroleum inventories (Class III), work stock, bench stock, dining facility plastic table wares are operating supplies. Operating supplies are GFM. 

3.1.2.2. The Contractor’s updated PCP shall address the internal and external actions to be taken by the Contractor for lost (loss), stolen (theft), damaged, or destruction (destroyed) (LTDD) of Government property. The Contractor shall establish and implement an effective system of internal controls to ensure physical accountability and authorized use of Government property to prevent LTDD. The Contractor shall report LTDD to the COR immediately upon discovery. The Contractor shall initiate corporate, internal investigations in to LTDD per Federal law, Contractor’s policies, and their AFCAP Management Plan [CDRL A001]. The Contractor shall not recommend formal Government investigations such as Report of Survey (ROS) or Financial Liability Investigations of Property Loss (FLIPL). Contractor shall not initiate ROS or FLIPL. Contractor shall support all Government investigations of property LTDD. All corporate investigations’ findings, photographs, documents, evidence, materiel, and recordings shall be made available to the Government’s investigators upon demand. The Contractor shall return all GFP and CMGP to the Government representative in the same quantity as received at the end of the PoP of this TO. The Contractor shall provide training to employees on proper use, disposal, and protection of Government property throughout the PoP. 

3.1.2.3. The Contractor PMS shall be detailed within the updated PCP. 

3.1.3. Government Furnished Material (GFM): GFM is not CMGP. GFM is excluded from personal property physical administration. The Contractor shall manage GFM using Federal and local regulations. The Contractor shall delineate the system and process of management of GFM to assure segregation of GFM records from personal property records within the Contractor’s updated PCP. GFM is not required to be listed in the Contractor’s PMS if the Contractor is accounting for it in a separate non-Governmental system or a separate Government system. If the Contractor is accounting for GFM (order-receipt-issue-inventory-adjustments) in a Contractor’s system, the Contractor system may be stand-alone or integrated within the Contractor’s PMS only if personal property listed in the Contractor’s PMS is clearly segregated from GFM as either GFP or CMGP. 

3.1.4. The Contractor personnel shall comply with prevailing Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), applicable unit SOPs, the local base and airfield operations regulations and licensing course, and if applicable and not in conflict with the TO, applicable U.S. Air Force instructions while utilizing or operating equipment items available for use by the Contractor, as applicable. 

3.1.4.1. The Contractor shall report all before-during-after use vehicle and equipment discrepancies per prevailing SOPs or instructions and assist in operators-level maintenance tasks such as washing, cleaning, changing light bulbs and wiper blades, checking fluid levels and topping off, fueling as permitted, checking tire pressures, performing safety checks, and executing operator’s level maintenance documents per local procedures or SOPs while utilizing or operating AEL items as applicable. The Contractor shall recommend changes to local forms, documents, or proposed SOP/process improvements to the Government via the ACO. 

3.1.4.2. The Contractor shall immediately report safety-of-use discrepancies to the Government while inspecting, utilizing, or operating items immediately upon discovery. 

3.1.4.3. The Contractor shall immediately report accidents to the unit representative, ACO, and PCO. The Contractor personnel shall support and comply with all safety/accident investigations. The Contractor shall initiate Contractor corporate internal investigations as required. The Contractor shall follow prevailing Department of Defense (DoD) regulations and procedures for contacting U.S. Government personnel in conjunction with obtaining investigation statements or any documents. 

3.1.4.4. The Contractor shall not operate safety dead lined equipment. 

3.1.5. Workload: The subject of the Contractor's task(s). Workload lists may or may not denote CMGP. Not all workload qualifies as CMGP—only workload requiring detailed inventory management is classified as CMGP and can be found at Appendix D. Workload is Government property, to include real property, which is the subject of the contract, being administratively managed as a service, inspected for serviceability, and/or repaired by Contractor as a service. Workload items may or may not be GFP, may or may not be CMGP, may or may not be exempt, and may or may not be reportable, contract property. Workload items have a contractually required action to be performed by the Contractor such as “inspect”, “service”, and “repair.” Workload items may include CAP. Acquisition of property by the Contractor during PoP may cause the workload to change. 

3.1.5.1. Examples of workload management: The Contractor shall inventory non-exempt workload CMGP in the Contractor’s PMS. The Contractor may elect to use Contractor’s PMS or another method of administration to manage exempt workload. The Contractor shall clearly identify and segregate all exempt workload items/lists that are being managed within the Contractor’s PMS. 

3.1.5.2. Transition Technical Inspections (TI) and Reporting: 

3.1.5.2.1. The incoming Contractor shall coordinate with the COR and outgoing Contractor on all completed and pending TI. The incoming Contractor shall immediately begin replacements, repairs and maintenance on all workloads. The incoming Contractor shall complete needed TI’s and request disposition from the Government within 15 calendar days of the Contractor’s Task 

Order Responsibility Date (CTORD) with zero discrepancies allowed. The Government will provide the requested disposition(s) within 15 calendar days. 

3.1.5.2.2. During the last 30 calendar days of PoP, applicable TIs shall be performed by the incumbent Contractor IAW PWS annex(s) that list WORKLOAD of all equipment to be maintained/repaired. The TIs shall be completed in time to coordinate the results with the incoming Contractor and COR during the Transition time. Workload may be GFP or stand-alone workload. The transition effort shall include the joint review of the inspections and a Transition Maintenance report submitted by the incoming contractor to the COR within five (5) calendar days of CTORD. 

3.1.5.3. The Contractor shall manage expendable workload items for use and replacement cost using any commercially recognized manner to include detailed local record-keeping. The Contractor shall manage exempt workload items (Example: billeting furniture and small appliances) as the subject of the TO as workload using any commercially recognized manner to include detailed local record-keeping. The Contractor may elect to use Contractor’s PMS or another method of administration to manage exempt workload. The Contractor shall clearly identify and segregate all exempt workload items/lists that are being managed within Contractor’s PMS. 

3.1.6. Contractor Acquired Property (CAP): Should Contractor gain approval for CAP, the Contractor shall contact the AFCAP Property Administrator for GFP compliance training and initiate reporting of GFP via the GFP Attachment. 

3.1.7. TO CMGP Disposal Services: Contractors performing services on an AFCAP contract with CMGP may perform property turn-in services under the guidance of the supported unit via the ACO. The Contractor is not authorized to provide turn-in, sales, or disposal services for Government property that is not associated with this TO. The Contractor shall continually identify excess and unserviceable equipment to the ACO and support the Government by preparing turnin documentation for the life of the TO. The Contractor shall perform routine turn-in procedures to assure that only limited amounts of unserviceable equipment as end items or scraps are accumulated. The Contractor shall develop detailed lists of recommended items for disposal and submit to the ACO for disposition approval and instructions by the Requiring Activity. 

3.1.8. Disposal of Government Property: The Contractor shall coordinate disposal or turn-in of Government property associated with the TO with the Approving Official (AO), Resource Advisor or owning unit and Defense Logistics Agency Disposition Services (DLADS) as applicable. Contractor shall provide training to employees on proper disposal of Government property of any value throughout the PoP. The Contractor shall manage and record disposition and disposal. Contractor shall obtain Government approval for, arrange for, support, administer, or execute the transportation of equipment to turn-in point/location. The Contractor shall turn in all excess or unserviceable/obsolete equipment associated with the TO within ten (10) calendar days after receipt of disposition instructions or as directed by the ACO. The Contractor shall clearly mark and segregate Government property awaiting turn-in. Contractor shall de-tag all property being turned in for disposal at the turn-in point. The Contractor shall salvage property tags whenever possible and include them with the turn-in documents. The Contractor shall not de-identify military equipment or remove data plates of any type from any type of equipment, military or commercial. 

The Contractor shall deliver turn-in documents to the Government and maintain copies for the term of the TO. The Contractor shall transfer all turn-in documents back to the Government at the time of TO closeout. The Contractor shall return all Government property to the Government representative in the same quantity and condition as received, minus normal wear and tear, at the end of the PoP of this TO or produce turn-in documentation. 

3.1.9. Property Disposition for TO Close Out: The Contractor shall provide the ACO a verified listing of Government property listed in the Contractor’s PMS and obtain disposition instructions NLT 30 calendar days prior to the conclusion of this TO in support of contract transition or closeout. The Contractor shall support TO close out. The Contractor shall complete $100 \%$ inventory and identify excesses items. The Contractor shall tag and provided a consolidated list of unserviceable equipment and parts on order. The Contractor shall support the Government by preparing turn-in documentation. The Contractor shall act in good faith to support Government or Contractor personnel during transition to a new service provider or back to Government operations. The Contractor shall plan staffing to support efficient TO close out of property records. 

3.1.10. The Contractor shall ensure their field managers are aware of and utilize the AFCAP Property Administrator (PA) to support timely, accurate recordkeeping and contract compliance. The AFCAP PA is a resource for all AFCAP Contractor TO’s and corporate staff. The Contractor shall schedule telephonic or in-person training from the AFCAP PA within 90 calendar days of beginning of PoP. The Contractor shall maintain the current version of the AFCAP Contractor’s Government Property Guidebook on site. 

3.2. Government Furnished Facilities (GFF): The Government will furnish work space of a reasonable size for each Contractor employee. Contractor personnel shall share office space with Government employees. 

3.2.1. Office and Workshop Space: The Government will furnish or make available in the reasonable vicinity of the work site(s) office spaces (desk, chair, file cabinet, file storage, telephone, and LAN computer connection capabilities) and workshop spaces for shared use by Contractor personnel. The Government will furnish a computer for Contractor personnel per allocated office space. The Government will make file storage (hanging and/or flat files) available in the reasonable vicinity of the office spaces. Workshop spaces may be in combination with office spaces or storage for Government furnished items. The Contractor shall remedy improper use of office or workshop space within 72 hours of COR and ACO recognition and notification to Contractor. 

3.2.2. Storage Space: The Government will furnish storage space for all equipment, supplies, and materials. All storage requests must be approved through the local siting and space utilization process. The Contractor is expected to utilize the storage space to optimize efficiency of use. The COR will conduct inspections of existing

3.1.9. Property Disposition for TO Close Out: The Contractor shall provide the ACO a verified listing of Government property listed in the Contractor’s PMS and obtain disposition instructions NLT 30 calendar days prior to the conclusion of this TO in support of contract transition or closeout. The Contractor shall support TO close out. The Contractor shall complete $100 \%$ inventory and identify excesses items. The Contractor shall act in good faith to support Government or Contractor personnel during transition to a new service provider or back to Government operations. The Contractor shall plan staffing to support efficient TO close out of property records. 

3.1.10. The Contractor shall ensure their field managers are aware of and utilize the AFCAP Property Administrator (PA) to support timely, accurate recordkeeping and contract compliance. The AFCAP PA is a resource for all AFCAP Contractor TO’s and corporate staff. The Contractor shall schedule telephonic or in-person training from the AFCAP PA within 90 calendar days of beginning of PoP. The Contractor shall maintain the current version of the AFCAP Contractor’s Government Property Guidebook on site. 

3.2. Government Furnished Facilities (GFF): The Government will furnish work space of a reasonable size for each Contractor employee. Contractor personnel shall share office space with Government employees. 

3.2.1. Office and Workshop Space: The Government will furnish or make available in the reasonable vicinity of the work site(s) office spaces (desk, chair, file cabinet, file storage, telephone, and LAN computer connection capabilities) and workshop spaces for shared use by Contractor personnel. The Government will furnish a computer for Contractor personnel per allocated office space. The Government will make file storage (hanging and/or flat files) available in the reasonable vicinity of the office spaces. Workshop spaces may be in combination with office spaces or storage for Government furnished items. The Contractor shall remedy improper use of office or workshop space within 72 hours of COR and ACO recognition and notification to Contractor. 

3.2.2. Storage Space: The Government will furnish storage space for all equipment, supplies, and materials. All storage requests must be approved through the local siting and space utilization process. The Contractor is expected to utilize the storage space to optimize efficiency of use. The COR will conduct inspections of existing storage space prior to additional storage space approvals. The Government has the right to refuse additional storage requests if the COR or ACO deems the request as not justified. 

3.2.3. Vehicle Parking Area: Upon request, the Government will provide overnight vehicle parking areas for Contractor and Sub-Contractor vehicles. Vehicles parked in these areas are parked at the risk of the Contractor. 

3.2.4. Request for Facility Modification: The Contractor shall not alter facilities without an approved AF Form 332 Base Civil Engineer Work Request and specific written permission from the COR. The Contractor shall provide a draft AF Form 332, project Scope of Work, itemized estimate of project costs, Gantt chart project timeline plan, and initial drawings of proposed facility changes at Contractor expense. The Contractor shall deliver proposed changes to a project package to the COR. The COR will return incomplete project packages to the Contractor for further action. 

3.2.5. Return of GFF: The Contractor shall return facilities to the Government in the same condition as received, except for fair wear and tear and approved modifications. 

3. 2.6. Use of GFF: The Contractor shall only use GFF in the performance of this TO. The Contractor shall designate a facility manager and alternate IAW Air Force requirements applicable to all organizations occupying all or part of a facility. 

3.2.7. Request for Additional GFF: The Contractor may request additional GFF by submitting a written justification to the COR. 

3.3. Real World Chemical Warfare Defense Equipment (CWDE) and Individual Protective Equipment (IPE) will be issued at one of the Expeditionary Theater Distribution Centers (ETDCs) or Expeditionary Logistics Readiness Squadron (ELRS) IPE Flights. The Contractor shall ensure employees assigned to AUAB or AASAB receive their CWDE/IPE at the ETDC. The Contractor shall ensure employees assigned to MSAB or ADAB receive their CWDE/IPE at the local ELRS IPE Flight. The Contractor shall ensure employees assigned to PSAB transit through AUAB and receive their CWDE/IPE from the AUAB ETDC prior to onward movement to PSAB. Note: Onward movement to PSAB must be via MILAR due to restrictions on military gear being carried/shipped through Doha International Airport, Qatar. All CWDE/IPE issued must be returned to the issuing ETDC or IPE Flight. LOA and proof of gas mask fit test are required for CWDE gear to be issued from the ETDC or IPE Flight. Note: The Government may elect to issue CWDE to employees assigned to AUAB, AASAB, ADAB and MSAB only when threat conditions warrant. Employees assigned to PSAB will be issued both CWDE and IPE and hand-carry all issued gear to PSAB. The Contractor shall ensure all employees, regardless of location, receive IPE. 

3.3.1. CWDE and PPE/IPE Equipment Accountability: Employees who permanently depart from a task for any reason (to include transfer to another task order) shall turn in ETDC equipment to the applicable issuing location IAW the Contractor’s procedures before departing employment and/or theater (or transfer of the equipment under the new employer). The USG will not be issue additional ETDC equipment until the prior equipment has been turned in or reimbursement for full replacement provided. The Contractor’s Property Management Plan shall identify how the equipment will be accounted for, secured and returned, particularly in cases where the Site Manager takes possession of the equipment of departing/departed personnel. The Contractor shall include accountability and ensure the correctness of individual hand receipts in all equipment inventories. The Contractor shall also maintain proper accountability for the equipment issued to individuals to reflect the total of each item issued to personnel. The Contractor shall update the master accountability document of this equipment monthly, during demobilization and within 30 calendar days of any task order personnel change. The Contractor shall provide a copy of the inventory to the AFCAP Deployment Specialist located at AFCAP Program Management Office Tyndall AFB. The Contractor shall ensure the updated information agrees with and is consistent with the individual hand receipts. The Contractor shall reimburse the Government within ten (10) calendar days of Government notification for the cost of equipment not accounted for or any equipment not returned in serviceable condition. 

3.3.2. Temporary Measures. For any reason, the Government may enact temporary measures to ship CWDE and PPE/IPE to the duty location. The Contractor shall provide a copy of the gas mask fit test and equipment sizes electronically (via e-mail) upon Government request. Shipment of this equipment will remain under positive Government control since the gas mask is an Arms Export Controlled item and cannot be shipped via standard commercial carriers. As soon as the reason for temporary measures has passed and restrictions are lifted, the Contractor shall resume pick-up and return of this equipment to the ETDC or IPE Flight. 

3.4. . Contractor Support Services: Contractor personnel may be required to obtain on-base driver’s licenses to operate Government-owned or Contractor provided vehicles on the Installation. The Government will provide this as well as Flight Line drivers training (as required), Off–Base Driver’s Training, Escort Training (if required), and Military Driver’s License Training. 

3.5. Government Furnished Services: U.S. currency (cash) will no longer be accepted at the Army & Air Force Exchange Service (AAFES), Morale, Welfare, and Recreation (MWR)/Services and Post Offices at AUAB, ADAB, AASAB, MSAB, PSAB in Southwest Asia; nor at Air Base 201, Agadez, Niger, Africa. Payment at these facilities can be made via debit or credit cards. See Appendix C entitled “Government Furnished Life Support and Services.” If not specified in the Appendix, the Contractor shall be responsible for all other required services. 

4.0. GENERAL INFORMATION: The following paragraphs are general in nature. Specifics will be found in the service area appendices. 

4.1. Contractor Communications Coordination: The Contractors shall furnish their own commercial telephones as needed (e.g., cell phones). The Contractor shall provide cellular phone service with text and photo capability for all key personnel at a minimum. 

4.2. Contractor’s Subcontract Management: The Contractor shall effectively monitor and provide oversight to all subcontractors’ performance on this TO. The Contractor shall submit in the Monthly Status Report (CDRL A004), how the subcontractor(s) were monitored, what was monitored, with what frequency, results of the monitoring (such as compliance with what standards), noted deficiencies and corrective actions taken with timelines for each correction or deficiency identified. The Contractor shall obtain all necessary badges and passes for subcontractor(s) and vehicles from the HN and US Officials prior to the commencement of work. The Contractor is responsible for the work and conduct of all subcontractors 

4.4. Operational Readiness and Deployment Requirements: The Contractor shall comply with all individual travel clearance requirements outlined in the FCG for their destination, regardless of transportation method. This includes obtaining necessary country, theater, and special clearances and confirming compliance with pre-travel requirements such as medical evaluations, immunizations, and Combatant Commander (CCDR)-mandated training. The FCG specifies current requirements and exceptions. Personnel traveling on specific order types must ensure their orders include any statements mandated by the FCG. All DoD personnel with security clearances traveling to foreign locations must complete pre- and post-travel security briefings and debriefings, even if not specified in Aircraft and Personnel Automated Clearance System (APACS) documentation. 

4.4.1. Fitness for Duty and Medical/Dental Limitations for USNs (prime and any subcontractors as required at the contingent location): The Contractor shall provide the ACO/COR on a one time basis within five (5) calendar days after CTORD, a letter certifying that all Contractor employees possess immunizations, and medical and dental screenings that meet Department of State (DOS) and DoD requirements for contractor personnel performing duty in the Area of Responsibility (AOR) [CDRL A015]. The Contractor shall also submit similar certification for employees hired between the start and finish of the PoP within five (5) calendar days of the new hire’s start date. The Contractor shall provide an annual certification to the ACO/COR, NLT one year after the initial CTORD certification date, verifying that all deployed personnel continue to meet DOS and DoD immunization, medical, and dental screening requirements. This annual certification shall include documentation of any updates or changes to personnel medical status. . 

4.4.2. Health Screening: Health Screening: The Contractor shall provide medical screening and vaccination requirements for all contractor employees IAW the AFCAP V Basic Contract Section 4.20.6. Contractors shall ensure employees receive typhoid and Hepatitis A vaccinations and shall ensure this information is documented in the employees’ medical record / screening documentation. Immunizations shall be maintained as outlined in the FCG for the applicable countries and shall be done at the contractor’s expense. The sole exceptions to this policy are anthrax and smallpox vaccines, if required, which will be provided at the Government’s expense. 

4.5. Hazardous Material (HAZMAT): The Contractor shall fully comply with installation Air Force, AFCENT and Host Nation HAZMAT requirements. The Contractor shall ensure all personnel who use or may be exposed to HAZMAT complete Hazard Communication training offered by the installation within two (2) weeks after the start of CTORD and shall renew their training annually thereafter. The Contractor shall accomplish AF Enterprise Environmental, Safety, and Occupational Health Management Information System (EESOH-MIS) training within one month of CTORD start date, and as required due to employee turnover. All personnel obtaining refrigerants or servicing refrigerant-containing appliances are required to be certified in accordance with Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) certification standards described in 40 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 82. Refrigerant technicians certified in accordance with alternative, internationally recognized standards are authorized to perform service, repair and maintenance where the certification is substantially equivalent with 40 CFR Part 82, Subpart B. The installation environmental officer is the delegated authority to determine whether alternative training is substantially equivalent to an EPA-approved certification program. The Contractor shall maintain Safety Data Sheets and the mandatory authorization (Form AF 3952) for hazardous chemicals used or stored in the workplace. 

4.6. The Contractor shall acknowledge all discrepancies and valid complaints within 24 hours of receipt. The Contractor shall achieve full resolution of those discrepancies and complaints within 48 hours of the initial acknowledgment. 

4.7. Personnel Qualifications: The PCO and ACO reserve the right to review personnel qualifications. Contractor shall ensure employees’ possess a Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Airframe/Powerplant license, or DD Form 214 with documentation reflecting aircraft maintenance experience. 

4.8. Communications: 

4.8.1. Cellular Phones. The Contractors shall furnish their own commercial telephones as needed. The Contractor shall provide cellular phones for the SM and ASM to ensure communication capability with the COR and ACO. 

4.8.2. Global Positioning System (GPS): Contractor cellular phones will have GPS capabilities disabled. 

4.8.3. Cellular Phone Approval (USN Employees Only): USNs with LOA’s and CAC cards are authorized to use cell phones in the performance of their official duties. 

4.8.4. Land Mobile Radios: The Contractor shall provide all radios or communication equipment needed for the TO, except those provided by the Government as GFP/CMGP, as identified in Appendix D. Any radio or communication requirements beyond what is provided as GFP/CMGP are the responsibility of the Contractor, at their own expense. The Contractor shall submit all nonGovernment radio requests, with full technical specifications, to the COR and installation’s frequency manager for approval before use. 

5.0. PERSONNEL QUALIFICATIONS. Key personnel, as listed in the table below, must possess experience in their respective functional areas and meet the minimum qualifications detailed below. See the applicable appendices for any specialized qualifications required for each functional service area. All USN personnel assigned to this TO shall have a SECRET security clearance and must obtain a Geneva Convention CAC. 

6.1. Operating Hours: Normal Hours of Operation are 24/7/365 coverage. 

6.2. Holidays: Due to mission requirements, work performance may be required during U.S. Federal holidays or during holidays of the HN. During American and HN holidays, the Contractor shall provide stand-by personnel when coordination with the COR and ACO indicates performance of service will not be required. One week prior to the holiday, the Contractor shall provide written notification to both the COR and ACO for approval. The notification shall identify by name which personnel will be on stand-by and a contact number. Stand-by personnel shall be available at all times and shall arrive at the installation within one (1) hour after being contacted. 

7.0. BASE ACCESS BADGING PROCESS. 

7.1. Installation Security/Passes/Identification: The Contractor shall follow existing security procedures and instructions applicable to the installation at all times. The Contractor shall notify the installation Security Forces Squadron personnel in writing NLT 15 calendar days after the TO award date and provide them with the TO number, start date, and service to be performed. The Contractor shall obtain personnel identification passes for all personnel and vehicle passes for all contract vehicles requiring entry onto the installation from the Government. The Contractor must complete a Contractor/Vendor Access List and submit it through the ACO for signature to the Security Forces and Pass and Registration office. To register vehicles, the Contractor shall present vehicle registration, proof of insurance and a valid driver’s license. The Government will not be liable for costs if contract personnel are denied base access due to security reasons beyond Government control. 

8.0. CONTRACTOR LN/OCN CELL PHONE/ELECTRONIC REQUEST PROCESS. Reserved.
