APPENDIX A 

ACRONYMS, ABBREVIATIONS AND DEFINITIONS 

1. Aerospace Ground Equipment (AGE). Equipment necessary for the recovery, maintenance, inspection, servicing or launching of aircraft. 

2. Administrative Contracting Officer (ACO): The duly appointed Government agent authorized to administer contracts and perform the day-to-day administration of the contract. The ACO is responsible for resolving administrative issues and negotiating equitable adjustments related to this TO. The PCO has delegated ACO appointment authority to the United State Air Force Central Command A48 office. 

3. American-made: (i) manufactured or produced in the United States, (ii) by an entity that is based in the United States, and (iii) predominantly using ingredients that were grown, mined, manufactured, or produced in the United States. 

4. Arrival Services. Those services required for preparing for and properly parking transient aircraft in an assigned parking area. 

5. Contractor-Acquired Property (CAP): Property that a contractor purchases, fabricates, or otherwise obtains to perform a government contract where the government takes ownership (title). If cost-plus funds are used to acquire property, it automatically becomes Government Property. Contractors manage CAP according to FAR Part 45. 

6. Contractor-Managed Government Property (CMGP): In the AFCAP context, CMGP refers to Government Property provided to the Contractor in two potential categories: "Shared Property" or "Workload." (Note that not all workload qualifies as CMGP—only workload requiring detailed inventory management is classified as CMGP.) CMGP is managed through the Contractor's PMS and is separate from GFP. The Contractor must collaborate with the Government to verify CMGP inventory using the same standards applied to GFP verification. All property disposal must be conducted IAW the Contractor's PMS and as directed or authorized by the ACO. While the Government retains ownership responsibility for CMGP, the Contractor is responsible for physical inventory and day-to-day management according to the same standards required for GFP under FAR Part 45. 

a. CMGP Shared Property: Property shared between the Government (or other identified parties) and the Contractor. The Contractor has primary usage rights to this equipment. While the Government may require access to the equipment, particularly at austere locations with limited availability, the Contractor's operational needs will be prioritized. The Government and the Contractor shall collaborate to develop a mutually agreeable schedule. However, in cases of conflicting needs, the Contractor's schedule will take precedence, ensuring minimal disruption to their work. For example, when sharing a crane for deliveries, the Contractor's scheduled usage will be prioritized, subject to reasonable accommodation of urgent Government requirements. 

b. CMGP Workload Property: Items, such as gym equipment, light-carts, etc., for which the Contractor performs maintenance or other services. 

7. Contractor’s Task Order Responsibility Date (CTORD): Official date the contractor becomes fully responsible for all task order requirements. Normally occurs the day after transition ends. This term applies only toservice task orders. 

8. Customer: The Government entity(ies) who require(s) construction, commodities or services to be provided by the AFCAP contract. Has ability to state what requirements are needed to support the mission(s).A.3. Departure Services. Those services required to assist the aircraft commander with launch procedures. 

9. Distinguished Visitors Codes 1-7. Civilian and military personnel in the ranks of the President through Colonel. 

10. Equipment: Any government property item on a TO for which the Contractor is responsible for accountability and/or must be inventoried by the Contractor. This includes all recreational items, particular those checked/signed out to users/customers/clients and expected to be returned in working/functioning order but for fair wear and tear during use. 

11. Follow-Me Services. Leading the aircraft with a "follow-me" vehicle to or from the runway and parking before takeoff or after landing. Also may include leading an aircraft with a "follow-me" vehicle from one parking spot to a new parking spot. The aircraft may be taxiing or being towed. 

12. Foreign Object (FO). A term commonly used to describe objects which if not removed from within or around an aircraft could cause, or have potential to cause, internal or external damage to aircraft and engines. 

13. Government-Furnished Property (GFP): Property in the possession of, or directly acquired by, the Government and subsequently furnished to the contractor for performance of a contract. Government-furnished property includes, but is not limited to, spares and property furnished for repair, maintenance, overhaul, or modification. Government-furnished property also includes CAP if the CAP is deliverable under a cost contract when accepted by the Government for continued use under the contract (FAR 45.101). 

14. Hot Cargo. An aircraft with explosive or hazardous cargo on board. 

15. Hung Ordnance. A munitions item that meets release parameters, but has failed to separate from the aircraft following an attempt to release it. The government will provide this training at AUAB. 

16. Maintenance Operations Center (MOC). This activity monitors all maintenance and servicing of aircraft and related support equipment 

17. Major Maintenance. Any extensive maintenance requiring additional technical orders, 

qualified personnel or equipment. 

18. Minor Maintenance. Maintenance related to servicing and inspecting and not considered major in nature. 

19. Official Business Only (OBO). The airfield is closed to all transient military aircraft for obtaining routine services such as fueling, passenger drop off or pickup, practice approaches, parking, etc. Aircrews and aircraft may use the airfield if official government business (including civilian) must be conducted on or near the airfield and prior permission is received from the airfield manager. 

20. Period of Performance $( \mathbf { P o P } )$ : Dates defining the beginning and ending of contractor performance; the start of PoP includes the date whereofficial performance assessments can be issued (i.e. CARs & PARs can be issued). 

21. Prior Permission Request (PPR). Airport is closed to transient aircraft unless approval for operation is obtained from the appropriate commander through Chief, Airfield Management or Airfield Operations Officer. PPR must be requested and confirmation received before the flight departs to that airfield. The purpose of PPR is to control volume and flow of traffic rather than to prohibit it. Prior permission is required for all aircraft. 

22. Program Manager (PM): Supervisor of a group of projects managing program risks outcome. 

23. Processing Services. Those services required to prepare transient aircraft to safely continue the mission. 

24. Red X Condition. A red X indicates that the weapon system, support system, or equipment is considered unsafe or unfit for flight or use and that the weapon system, support system, or equipment shall not be flown or used until the unsatisfactory condition is corrected or symbol is cleared. 

25. Stakeholder: A person or group that has an investment, share, or interest in products or services being provided by the AFCAP contract. For example, AFCENT is a stakeholder and also the “clearinghouse” for every task order that supports an Air Force tasked mission in the Southwest Asia Theater of Operations. 

26. Transient Aircraft. Any aircraft not assigned to a base, that are en route from one location to another that may require routine servicing. However, aircraft deploying to or flying missions from a base with a squadron of that Mission Design Series (MDS) assigned to the base, are not considered transient aircraft. The squadron assigned to that base will be responsible for the aircraft, with or without necessary maintenance support from the home base. Depot and/or modification input aircraft may also be defined as transient aircraft. C-21 aircraft assigned to AUABreturning from a mission are considered transient aircraft unless terminating for the night. 

27. Transient Aircraft Maintenance Management and Control. These services apply to management, control and preparation for aircraft. 

28. Transition: The period of time the incoming contractor works side-by-side with the outgoing contractor to ensure task continuity. On the first day of transition, the incoming contractor shall be fully resourced as proposed (TaskOrder Management Plan that is incorporated into the Task Order) (e.g. fully staffed, equipped, all materials). This term applies only to service task orders 

29. User Inspection or Servicing. That inspection accomplished to insure serviceability and safety of the support equipment prior to each use. It consists of a visual inspection for obvious defects and adequate servicing of fuel, oil, hydraulic fluid, water, battery fluid, leaks and tires. 

30. Workload: Government property for which the contractor performs maintenance or other services 

APPENDIX B 

PUBLICATIONS AND FORMS 

B1. Publications and forms that apply to the PWS are listed below. The publications are coded as mandatory (M) or advisory (A). The Contractor shall comply with (M) or be guided by (A) publications and shall use those forms to the extent necessary to accomplish requirements in this PWS. At the start of the contract, the Government will provide hard copies of all publications and forms which are not available via public internet web locations, as specified in this appendix. The Contractor shall ensure the most current publications are utilized. Supplements or amendments to listed publications from any organizational level may be issued during the life of the contract. The COR will notify the Contractor of any publication change and will forward supplements or amendments to listed publications and forms that are not available on these web sites. The Government will continue to supply the Government forms needed for daily operations. Upon completion of the contract, the Contractor shall return to the Government all issued publications and unused forms. NOTE: The Contractor shall furnish all of the NFPA standards listed in this appendix. 

B.3. (Department of the) Air Force Publications: Air Force publications and forms may be found at: https://www.e-publishing.af.mil/Product-Index/Select the “Departmental” link to access Department of the Air Force level publications or forms. Click on the appropriate selection button for forms or publications. The following publications are applicable to this effort and may be specifically cited in portions of the PWS, its appendices, annexes, and attachments. 

B.4. Department of Defense (DOD) Publications: DOD instructions and other publications may be found at https://www.esd.whs.mil/dd/. 

B.5. Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC): The UFC may be found in their entirety at: http://www.wbdg.org/ffc/dod/dod/unified-facilities-criteria-ufc 

B.6. Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) Standards: OSHA standards may be found at: http://www.osha.gov. 

B.7. Air Force Handbooks (AFH) and Military Handbooks (MIL HDBKs). Reserved. 

B.8. National Fire Protection Association (NFPA). Reserved. 

B.9. Technical Bulletins (TB) and Technical Orders (T.O.). 

Note: Enhanced Technical Information Management System (ETIMS) is the Air Force system for managing Technical Orders. It is available to everyone with an Air Force Portal account at https://www.my.af.mil/ to view any distribution A (Public Release) publications. Contact a Technical Order Distribution Office (TODO) to request access to distribution B, C, D, E, F publications. The COR will supply applicable access after task order award. 

B.10. Other Publications and Agreements.   
Note: Some publications are sensitive in nature and will only be provided upon award. CE Playbooks available at https://www.ceplaybooks.com/   
AFCEC Business Process Library (BPL) available at   
https://usaf.dps.mil/sites/11252/24048/CO%20Support%20TRIRIGA%20Deploy/TRIRIGA%2 0Ops%20Wiki/Home.aspx 

APPENDIX C 

GOVERNMENT FURNISHED LIFE SUPPORT and SERVICES 

C.1. The following is a summary of the type of support the Government intends to provide the contractor. There may be mission related exceptions, however, the Government intends to provide contractors these services at the same level as they are provided to military and DoD civilian personnel. In the event of any discrepancy between this summary and the description of services in the Statement of Work or Performance Work Statement, this appendix will take precedence. When contractor employees are in transit, all checked blocks are considered authorized. 

Other Country Nationals (OCN) Employees (Note #1) 

Note #1: Authorized Government Services does not necessarily mean that the Government provides these services to contractors on a non-reimbursable basis. For certain categories of authorized services, the contractor may be required to reimburse the Government IAW applicable Federal, DoD, or COCOM regulations/policies and/or the contract terms and conditions. 

Note #2: CCDR authorization is required IAW FAR Clause 52.225-26 in order for a contractor to be authorized to carry a weapon. 

Note #3: Contractors shall be billeted on the installation. No billeting standard can be guaranteed. Downgrades may occur with little or no notification if dictated by the mission. The billeting office will notify program/contracting offices if this occurs. Except for personnel located at Al Dhafra Air Base, United Arab Emirates (UAE), and Ali Al Salem Air Base, Kuwait, the Government will make available living quarters for each USN Contractor employee as needed. Contractor employees may be expected to live in tents with multiple occupants for all or a portion of this task order performance period. If less than a private room and bath is available, Contractor employees may elect to reside off base provided there is no cost to the contract (i.e., at the employee's expense), and mission readiness is not affected. If Contractor employees elect to establish a full time off-base residence, Government housing will not be provided unless recalled in support of the mission, including temporary "on call" support. In addition, during the most serious Force Protection Condition (FPCON), the Installation Commander may require employees to remain on base and will be lodged on base in lodging facilities. Living quarters will be subject to Fire Protection and Public Health inspections. 

Note #4: Hot meals may be reduced from three per day to less or none. MREs may be substituted for DFAC- provided meals. Contractors will receive the same meal standard as military and DoD civilian personnel. 

Note #5: Resuscitative/Emergency Care is the default level of care. Unless specifically negotiated in the contract, ALL medical care is reimbursable to the U.S. Government. 

Note #6: Other Remarks: Letters of Authorization to state, “Fuel Authorized for Official Use Only”. Laundry: The Government will permit full access for USN Contractors and US employees to any laundry services on the same basis as available to US military members. 

C.2. The type and amount of support that the US Embassy support, if any, must be coordinated in advance between the US Mission and the contracting agency in accordance with Department of State Foreign Affairs Handbook, 2-FAH-2. Contractors are not authorized to deploy personnel requiring US Mission support prior to receiving clearance from the contracting officer. The 

Government will not coordinate with the embassy on behalf a contract/program/contractor. The United States Embassy in Amman's main telephone line is 962-6-590-6000. 

C.3. Community-Base Support Services: The Government will permit full access for USN Contractors and US employees to all recreational, banking, resale, chaplain, morale telephone calls, and fitness related services on the same basis as available to US military members. The United Service Organizations (USO) is not a Government agency and USO services will be granted only to those contractors who meet USO criteria for service (e.g., US military retirees). 

C.3.1. The Contractor shall adhere to all installation regulations, policies, standards, and guidance, as applicable to the task order operations. 

C.4. Floor Plans: The Government will provide floor plans upon request to the SM, but has the right to deny floor plans to a facility due to security requirements. The SM has the responsibility to safe guard any provided floor plans. 

C.5. The Government will provide fuel (mogas and diesel) to support Contractor vehicles for official use only. Official use includes those activities that directly support the requirements of this PWS. When contractors are lodged off-base due to lodging non-availability or mission requirements, the commute to and from the base is considered official use and will be provided for commutes up to $1 0 0 ~ \mathrm { k m }$ (each way). Fuel is not authorized for contractor's personal use. Fuel consumption will be calculated at a rate of $2 7 \mathrm { k m / g a l }$ for tracking purposes. For bases that have implemented the VILKey system at the on-base station, contractor personnel will be issued VILKeys to refuel contractor vehicles. Contractor owned/leased vehicles includes contractor employee's owned/leased vehicles utilized for Official Use. Contractor shall provide the ACO/COR a vehicle usage log for each vehicle at the completion of the month documenting distance traveled for Official Use. 

C.5.1. Within 10 calendar days after CTORD, the contractor shall provide: 

(a) list of vehicles that will be used during the performance of the contract period to include year, make, model, and license plate number 

(b) the estimated monthly fuel usage, and fuel tank capacity for each vehicle to the ACO and the COR. The contractor shall report changes to the contractor’s vehicle fleet (additions, removals, or substitutions) to the ACO/COR within 10 calendar days of the change. 

C.5.2. Contractor Vehicle Fuel Report: Not later than the third day of each month, the contractor shall provide actual fuel consumption for contractor-owned vehicles for the previous month to the ACO and COR. The reports shall include: 

(a) a list of vehicles that used fuel during the month to include year, make, model, fuel type and license plate number 

(b) the actual number of gallons per vehicle used during the month (c) the date fuel was received for each vehicle during the month 

C.5.3. The contractor shall provide fuel use “estimates” each quarter to the ACO/COR, to include the fuel tank capacity for each vehicle. If the contractor determines that additional fuel is required, the contractor shall provide: 

(a) the new estimated monthly fuel usage and fuel tank capacity for each vehicle   
requiring the additional fuel   
(b) the reason for the additional fuel 

C.5.4. The following are prohibited uses of government fuel: 

(a) use for purposes other than executing requirements of the contract (or government   
contracts)   
(b) use of fuel in excess of what is needed to execute the requirements of the contract (or   
government contracts)   
(c) resale, exchange, or transfer 

C.5.5. If the contractor knowingly engages in any prohibited use of fuel, the contractor is liable to the government for the value of any government fuel beyond contract requirements and may be subject to contract termination
